title: Stewardship without walls: taming the unregulated antibiotic economy to contain antimicrobial resistance in Ethiopia
What electability means to voters and how it affects their decisions
abstract: Voters must often resolve dilemmas between choosing their favorite candidate and the one likeliest to win an election. Researchers know surprisingly little about how voters even conceptualize electability, let alone make these decisions. We use two novel survey experiments to evaluate how voters trade off policy agreement and electability in their decisions. The first experiment presents respondents with policy agreement between themselves and candidates and candidates’ electability rankings on the same scale, allowing us to directly compare. We show that while making electability information salient modestly reduces support for candidates with limited prospects, respondents remain primarily policy-motivated. Additionally, we show that respondents actually construe electability as a signal about a candidate’s qualifications rather than as a signal about getting votes.

title: Performing Identity: Repetition, Emoji, and Candidate Indicators of Amplified Visibility in Jordanian–Palestinian Facebook Discourse
authors: Alaeddin A. Banikalef, Mohammad Issa Mehawesh
abstract: This study examines how Arabic Facebook comments shape political tensions between Jordanians and Palestinians. The corpus contains 800 public comments collected from public Facebook pages focused on Jordanian–Palestinian affairs between January and July 2025. Methods combine immersive digital ethnography with systematic netnographic coding and a reproducible coordination–detection protocol applied at three scales: micro (linguistic strategies), meso (semiotic affordances), and macro (temporal and network patterns). The results show that dialectal markers, religious formulae, emojis, short slogans, and threaded replies interact to produce strong identity signaling and affective circulation. A subset of items displays heuristic markers associated with amplified visibility, such as exact-text repetition, clustered timestamps, repeated links, and low-engagement account profiles. These markers are presented as candidate indicators of amplification, not as proof of centralized orchestration. This study provides open detection scripts and appendix materials for human adjudication and follow-up network analyses. Findings point to low-cost interventions, such as prompts before verbatim reposting and triage workflows for flagged clusters, and to the need for cross-platform tracing and bot analyses to confirm provenance.

title: “My Non-Existing Organ”: Transitional Objects, Compensatory Romance, and the Affective Enclaves of Chinese Female Idol Fandom
abstract: This article examines how young Chinese female fans of the idol group ONER construct and defend gendered subjectivities through digitally mediated intimate practices across Weibo, Douban, and private WeChat groups. Drawing on 24 months of multi-sited digital ethnography across seven Chinese cities, the study documents two identificatory positions, the girlfriend-fan and the mum-fan, and two material-semiotic practices through which these identifications are enacted: coded language and cyber-penis memes . Building on Radway’s argument that women’s affective fandom constitutes psychic compensation for the unmet needs produced by patriarchal social structures, the study demonstrates that Chinese female fans engage in a comparable compensatory romance in response to the constraints imposed by China’s gender governance regime and state regulation of female fan culture. Ethnographic analysis reveals that girlfriend-fan and mum-fan positions are fluid, strategically negotiated stances through which fans manage the tensions between romantic fantasy, maternal retreat, and moral anxiety. Coded language and cyber-penis memes function as transitional objects in Winnicott’s sense, while Butler’s framework of phallic resignification reveals these memes as collective acts of symbolic resistance. Together, these practices constitute the affective enclave, a collectively defended potential space within China’s surveilled platform ecology in which non-normative female desire is articulated and protected.

title: Ex Ante Immigration Policy: How Local Politics Anticipate Future Flows
authors: Diana Da In Lee, James Bisbee
abstract: Scholarly work on the politics of immigration in the U.S. has focused primarily on the influence of local demographics. Less understood are the ways in which local governments respond to demographic changes beyond the city limits. We hypothesize that local governments anticipate future influxes of immigrants from neighboring localities and adjust policies ex ante in order to constrain their movement before it can occur. We predict variation in anti-immigrant legislation as a function of proximity to nearby immigrant populations and find that a 10 percentage point increase in the share of the foreign-born population in a city’s surrounding areas doubles the likelihood that a city considers a restrictive policy. The effect is more pronounced when neighboring immigrants originate from Latin America than from other regions. We also find suggestive evidence that knowledge of adjacent foreign-born populations is transmitted via commuting patterns as neighboring populations commute into cities for work. Our research highlights the importance of spatial networks in local politics and how local political units influence one another and engage in an anticipatory strategic response.
journal_full: Journal of Race, Ethnicity and Politics

title: Digital Voter Mobilization Strategies of a Black Woman-Led Organization in Brazil
abstract: This article examines the Rio-based nonpartisan organization, the Marielle Franco Institute’s digital strategies to appeal to Afro-Brazilian voters. The literature reveals that most Afro-Brazilian voters do not vote along racial lines. However, scholarship also reveals that politicians make racial appeals to Afro-Brazilian voters. I argue that the MFI uses digital media to mobilize Afro-Brazilian voters to support progressive political candidates. I find that it seeks to mobilize them in four ways. The first is the Marielle Franco Agenda, an interactive platform that features progressive political candidates with similar agenda issues as Franco. Second, they highlight political candidates that are practitioners of African-derived religions. The third is that it promotes coalition politics through its collaboration with another nonpartisan organization to promote progressive candidates, and lastly, the MFI highlights candidates with a pro-Black woman aesthetic that challenges the notion that political spaces are nonblack spaces.
journal_full: Journal of Race, Ethnicity and Politics

title: Polarized White Attitudes Toward Black Lives: Did The Party Do It?
abstract: Among White Americans, have attitudes toward the Black Lives Matter (BLM) movement become increasingly polarized between 2016 and 2020, and was that polarization sustained through 2024? This study conceptualizes BLM polarization as a growing divergence in attitudes toward the movement—both in overall evaluations and in widening gaps across key orientations such as partisanship, racial resentment, social dominance orientation (SDO), and authoritarianism. Building upon recent work on partisan polarization, I examine individual-level changes in racial attitudes toward BLM over an extended time period and determine if partisanship or other socio-psychological predispositions predict this change. Through the use of the American National Election Studies (ANES) 2016–2020–2024 panel data, I find that BLM polarization occurred from 2016 to 2020 and remained robust from 2020 to 2024. Racial resentment, SDO, and authoritarianism also served as important factors associated with BLM polarization, alongside party affiliation.
journal_full: Journal of Race, Ethnicity and Politics

title: Race over Riches: How Racial Solidarity Trumps Economic Self-Interest for African American Homeowners
abstract: American politics scholars have long argued for the centrality of Black group consciousness in political decision-making for African Americans, regardless of class. However, what has not been completely explored is whether there are specific circumstances in which class-based concerns are prioritized for middle-class African Americans over racial group considerations. This paper explores whether the specific circumstance of direct economic threat heightens the relevance of class considerations over racial group considerations for local redistributive policy preferences among Black homeowners. Utilizing a novel survey experiment, I directly pit economic self-interest against racial group solidarity to analyze the prioritized political consideration for affluent Black political decision-making. I argue that the strength of racial group solidarity will extend to African American homeowners foregoing their own economic self-interest, defined as their property value, for the benefit of the overall racial group. Findings support my hypotheses and demonstrate that Black homeowners higher in linked fate are most likely to show the highest levels of support for the redistributive policy when low-income tenants are explicitly described as Black, as compared to the White condition or the control. These findings point to the effects and non-effects of economic self-interest in Black political decision-making depending on the racialized context. Lastly, this inquiry points to the resilience of Black group racial solidarity and its role in the formation of Black policy preferences irrespective of class-based intra- group differences.

Defining Ethnic and Migrant Minorities in Survey Research across Europe: Insights from the Ethnic and Migrant Minorities Survey Registry
authors: Dimitrios Rafail Tservenis, Yuma Ando, Dimitri Prandner, Laura Morales, Ami Saji, Anikó Bernát, Mónica Méndez-Lago
abstract: Surveys of ethnic and migrant minorities are key sources of data and information on these populations. How such surveys define, identify, and operationalize these target populations is consequential for the findings and inferences resulting from their analysis. This article systematically identifies and assesses the diverse approaches used in these surveys to define and operationalize the studied ethnic and migrant minorities. Through a comprehensive analysis of survey-level metadata from the Ethnic and Migrant Minorities Survey Registry, covering 1,840 national and subnational surveys conducted between 2000 and 2021 across 34 European countries, we examine the survey questions used to classify the target groups, as well as the broader categorizations that they reveal. The paper highlights common practices and emerging trends in defining and operationalizing ethnic and migrant minority populations. The findings also inform future decision-making by survey researchers on how to define and measure ethnic/origin-related concepts.

title: Organizational structure of migrant brokerage: the case of investment immigration to the U.S.
Pathways to occupational inclusion in host societies
authors: Ida Drange, Astrid Gillespie, Line Nortvedt, Ali Osman, Marianne Teräs
abstract: Immigrants’ access to relevant jobs is crucial for social integration and better life chances. For migrants in regulated professions, occupational reintegration depends on accreditation of previous learning by national, sectorial authorities. These migrants face complex systems that both enable and hinder occupational reintegration. In this article, we examine skilled migrants’ pathways to their vocations in Norway and Sweden, with a special emphasis on the role of bridging programmes to this end. Our analysis is based on interviews with migrants who have enrolled in or completed occupational requalification programmes. We explore how institutional opportunity structures shape their access to their professions and use four case portraits to illustrate how programme pre-requisites, timing, location and life-circumstances affect participation, and how such programmes are adapted to the candidates’ life situations. The portraits also show how the two countries offer varying support for reacredditation: Norway’s system appears more formalized and less integrated across immigration statuses compared with the Swedish system. Migrants in both countries call for a more systematic and coordinated intersectoral approach from public authorities to facilitate access to licensed occupations.

title: Chinese diasporas in the Pacific Islands: a bilingual comparative interpretive review
Broadening participation: 20-year outcomes from undergraduate science training programs
authors: Anna Woodcock, Samantha D. Aguilar, Paul R. Hernandez, Mary E. Peterson, P. Wesley Schultz
abstract: Science thrives on diverse perspectives, yet women and scientists from underrepresented racial and ethnic groups remain underrepresented in doctoral-level biomedical research careers. Despite decades of investment in training initiatives, rigorous long-term evidence of their effectiveness is limited. We conducted a 20-year prospective, matched-control longitudinal study to test whether participation in two National Institutes of Health undergraduate programs, the Research Initiative for Scientific Enhancement (RISE) and Minority Access to Research Careers (MARC), increases doctoral (Ph.D.) completion. Using propensity score matching on 11 baseline characteristics, we followed 743 students from 2005 to 2025. Participants were more than twice as likely as matched peers to earn a Ph.D., with effects remaining robust after adjusting for prior academic achievement and career intentions. These findings demonstrate that sustained undergraduate training programs can durably expand participation in the biomedical research workforce.

title: The patronising city: adult-centrism and youth exclusion in urban environments
abstract: Achieving inclusive urban environments requires addressing systemic exclusion of young people. This paper introduces the concept of the “patronizing city” to explain how adult-centrism shapes political, social, and spatial domains of urban life. This paper describes an analytical framework linking governance, institutional practices, and built environments to adult-centered planning. Through illustrative cases, it advances debate on inclusion, spatial justice, and equity as critical dimensions of urban sustainability.

title: Holistic fairness considerations in facility placement decisions
authors: James Crabb, Stephanie L. Kane, Assefaw H. Gebremedhin

title: Exponential growth estimations and cognitive biases: Ability or availability?
authors: Ami Feder, Maayan Katzir, Eliran Halali, Michal Pinhas
abstract: Exponential growth is consistently underestimated, and this underestimation has traditionally been viewed as reflecting a stable, if poor, mathematical ability. In this investigation, we show that exponential growth estimation is also susceptible to cognitive biases. In a series of three pre-registered experiments (total N = 2,836), we found that estimations of exponential growth are affected by the process being estimated, with larger underestimation for recovery than for infection cases of coronavirus. This difference in estimation was not associated with emotional and behavioral factors such as concerns regarding the virus or general anxiety (Experiments 1–3). When estimating exponential growth in the stock market (Experiment 2), the exponential growth of estimations of a positive (i.e., gains) or a negative (i.e., losses) scenario did not differ from each other, suggesting that the consistent larger underestimations of recovery (i.e., positive) compared to infection (i.e., negative) does not reflect a framing effect. Rather, this underestimation difference reflected the availability heuristic, as the difference was larger for people who were exposed (via more exposure to liberal than conservative news media outlets) to discussions about the exponential growth of coronavirus infection (Experiment 3). The importance of realizing that exponential growth is vulnerable to cognitive biases, that are shaped by the discourse in news media outlets is discussed. Particularly, this vulnerability should be taken into consideration by world leaders, especially when relying on the public’s understanding of exponential growth to counteract other global crises that grow exponentially and have severe implications for human life.

title: Manual de introdução ao modelo de avaliação e intervenção familiar integrada para o encaminhado. Versão 2012
authors: Ana Teixeira de Melo, Madalena Alarcão
abstract: Este manuscrito contém o Manual de Apoio ao Encaminhador do Modelo de Avaliação e de Intervenção Familiar Integrada

title: Even More Unequal Childhoods: 45 Years of Widening Income Gaps in Parental Investments in the United States
abstract: Parents can convert economic resources into advantages and opportunities for their children through spending on goods, services, and experiences. Using data from the 1980–2024 Consumer Expenditure Survey, this study provides the most comprehensive account to date of income-group trends in parental financial investments in childcare, education, and extracurricular activities. Income gaps widened throughout these 45 years. Per-child spending in the top decile nearly quadrupled in real terms, while bottom-quartile spending was slightly lower in 2024 than in 1980. Thus, the top decile spent roughly three times as much per child as the bottom quartile in the early 1980s but more than thirteen times as much by 2024. This widening spans all three categories, is also reflected in the share of households’ total budgets directed toward spending on children, and is driven more by how much households spend than by whether they spend at all. These findings document a sustained widening of income gaps in parental financial investments and point to these expenditures as an increasingly important dimension of unequal childhoods.

title: Prediction Markets, Gamblification, and YOLO Capitalism
abstract: This paper examines prediction markets through the theoretical framework of YOLO capitalism, arguing that many major critiques of prediction markets reflect deeper transformations occurring within the digitally-mediated dimensions of late-stage capitalism. While prediction markets are often presented as efficient mechanisms for aggregating collective intelligence and forecasting future events, they increasingly operate within speculative environments shaped by virality, emotional participation, meme culture, and platformized finance. The paper analyzes five major critiques of prediction markets: manipulation and narrative distortion, insider informational asymmetries, perverse incentives surrounding catastrophe speculation, irrational crowd dynamics and memetic volatility, and the broader financialization of reality itself. It argues that prediction markets increasingly transform collective uncertainty into continuously tradable speculative and cultural commodities.

title: Reconstructing Visual Discourses at Scale: Integrating Unsupervised Clustering and Iconological Interpretation
abstract: Reconstructive approaches to visual analysis have historically operated through close interpretation of small case selections, which limits the analysis of visual practices at field level. This paper proposes a five-stage procedure that re-scales documentary picture interpretation to large visual corpora while preserving its reconstructive logic. The procedure substitutes embedding-based computational clustering for the first, descriptive stage of image analysis, retains iconological reconstruction as a fundamentally interpretive operation, and adds a distributional layer through which discursive patterns become traceable across a field of practice. Operating on a corpus of 73,206 images from 13,000 Tinder profiles, it moves through (1) embedding-based clustering, (2) cross-coding of image attributes, (3) iconological reconstruction of constructions of attractiveness, (4) profile-level clustering into repertoires, and (5) socio-genetic distributional analysis. The procedure shifts the object of reconstruction from the tacit orientations of individual picture producers to the discursive orders of knowledge collectively articulated across a field, and clarifies the principled division of labour between computation and interpretation that such a shift requires.

title: Beyond Thoughtless Reflexes: A Moderate Approach to the Quantification of Qualitative Data
abstract: The article proposes a critical analysis of how quantification is understood and used in social research. Extreme positions are often found in the specialized literature, which includes quantitative approaches that tend to quantify everything, transforming numbers into an end in themselves, as well as qualitative approaches that avoid or reject the use of numbers, viewing them as an inherent threat to understanding. The article advocates for a moderate approach, demonstrating that scientific knowledge exists even in the absence of counting, quantification, and measurement, but also that a reality described or explained without numbers is not necessarily thicker, but rather incomplete. Both the benefits of quantification and its major risks are explored and illustrated by examples drawn even from visual research. The article argues that numbers should not be eliminated a priori from sound research, but also that quantification must never be an automatism, but a rational, transparent, and fully assumed option. The researcher's expertise lies not in their paradigmatic radicalism, but rather, among other things, in their deliberate decisions regarding when, how, and why to quantify.

title: Gaging LLMs’ strengths and weaknesses in political content analysis
abstract: Recent innovation and growth in the capabilities of generative artificial intelligence have opened options for the scholarship of political communication. While large language models (LLMs) have much to offer to improve content analysis workflows in political communication, there is as of yet no consensus regarding which options deliver the best results to the field and scale best computationally. This paper investigates the performance of five distinct classification models across three content analysis tasks of varying complexity: identifying political content, classifying political issues, and detecting stance towards climate policies and movements in news articles. Our automated coding is validated by 36,320 individual human coding decisions. We show that (political) task complexity is a crucial determinant for model selection. While simpler, efficient models perform well on lower to moderate complexity tasks, both proprietary and commercial LLMs exhibit superior performance on the nuanced stance detection task when appropriately instructed. These findings underscore the need to balance performance gains with computational costs and CO2 emissions, advocating for a “frugality approach” to automated coding.

title: Extracurricular Activities and Educational Expectations of Children with Undocumented Backgrounds: Evidence from Los Angeles
abstract: Executive Summary The academic benefits of extracurricular participation for disadvantaged children are well known. However, whether such participation would promote educational expectations for children of undocumented families is unclear, given the formidable institutional barriers facing them. Using the Los Angeles Family and Neighborhood Study (L.A. FANS), we investigate the role of extracurricular activities in immigrant-origin youth’s academic expectations, focusing on children of undocumented immigrants. Major findings include the following: • While extracurricular participation is positively associated with educational expectations for US-origin children, the benefit is unclear for immigrant-origin youth. • Compared with US-origin children, extracurricular participation of any type (sports-oriented, and non-sports-inclusive) is associated with smaller gains in high school expectations for both children of documented and undocumented immigrants. • Compared with US-origin children, sports-oriented activities are associated with smaller gains in college expectations for children of undocumented immigrants. • The disparities were salient among children (ages 12-17) than among younger children (ages 9–11). These findings are consistent with segmented assimilation theory and racialized organizations theory, where structural inequalities embedded in extracurricular settings operate in ways that marginalize racial-ethnic minorities. To better serve these minoritized youth, practitioners should create more inclusive environment where mutual respect for racial and ethnic differences is established and maintained. More importantly, educational and immigration policies should address structural inequalities and legal barriers facing immigrant-origin youth.

title: Epistemic Filters in Survey and Platform Research: Sampling Visibility, Measurement Limits, and Uncertainty Communication in Media Studies
abstract: This paper develops epistemic filters as a conceptual-methodological framework for connecting survey inference, platform data visibility, measurement limits, and uncertainty communication in media and social research. The framework is grounded in established concerns in survey methodology, computational social science, construct validation, and public communication of uncertainty. It introduces the non-play-dice paradigm only as a bounded analogy: apparent stochasticity may arise when a researcher observes a projected subset of a more complex system. The physics analogy is used to clarify the logic of restricted observability, not to claim that social systems obey mechanical laws. In social research, epistemic filters operate through sampling frames, platform access, questionnaire design, nonresponse, weighting, factor analysis, and analytic modeling. The paper defines survey operators for sampling, measurement, and analysis; proposes a five-construct instrument as a validation agenda rather than an already validated scale; outlines reliability, construct-validation, platform-implementation, and Bayesian updating routines; and provides reproducible R scripts using simulated data for demonstration only. Applications are developed for polling, journalism, media literacy, digital trace research, misinformation studies, influencer economies, and hidden or low-visibility populations.

title: THE ROLE OF VERBAL AND NONVERBAL COMMUNICATION IN WIDYAISWARA'S TEACHING PERFORMANCE AT GOVERNMENT TRAINING INSTITUTIONS
authors: Hasdiyanto Hafied, Samson Laurens, Rido Dominggus Latuheru
abstract: This study examines the role of verbal and nonverbal communication in shaping the teaching performance of widyaiswara (government trainers) at state training institutions in Indonesia. Teaching performance in adult education contexts depends heavily on a trainer's communicative competence, yet the interplay between verbal clarity and nonverbal expressiveness remains under-examined in the Indonesian civil- service training environment. Using a descriptive qualitative approach, data were collected through in- depth interviews, non-participant observation, and document analysis involving widyaiswara and training participants at a regional training agency (Badan Pengembangan Sumber Daya Manusia). The findings indicate that verbal communication—encompassing language clarity, terminological accuracy, vocal variety, and responsive questioning—forms the cognitive backbone of effective instruction, while nonverbal communication—gesture, eye contact, proxemics, facial affect, and paralanguage—functions as the affective channel that sustains learner attention, builds credibility, and reinforces message retention. The two modalities operate complementarily: incongruence between them reduces source credibility and learner trust, whereas congruence strengthens andragogical engagement. The study concludes that widyaiswara teaching performance is best understood as an integrated verbal–nonverbal communicative practice and recommends communication-competence training as a core element of trainer certification.

title: Workforce determinants influencing regional public oral healthcare delivery in Queensland: A context-sensitive qualitative study
authors: Chaminda Jayasekara, Kelly McGowan, Nicolie Jenkins, Alexia Rohde, Sundresan Naicker
abstract: In Australia, adult public dental care is available to specific eligible populations and is limited to a specified range of services. Waiting times to access care can be compounded in regional and peri-urban areas by workforce shortages, isolation and socioeconomic disadvantage. Limited evidence on workforce perspectives in these settings hinders targeted solutions. This study addresses this gap by examining factors that enable or constrain workforce performance in a regional oral healthcare service. It aims to identify and map the context-specific barriers and enablers affecting the oral health workforce in delivering care for eligible adults within a regional Hospital and Health Service in Queensland, Australia. A cross-sectional qualitative study was undertaken using semi-structured interviews to explore workforce determinants. Participants were purposively selected from diverse workforce roles. Interviews were conducted in person, online or via telephone, using a theory informed guide. Data were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim and analysed using a hybrid inductive-deductive approach. Reflexive thematic analysis identified emergent themes, which were subsequently mapped to TDF domains through content analysis. Twenty-eight participants representing clinical, administrative, support and leadership roles were interviewed. Inductive analysis identified five overarching themes: perceptions of community and patients; communication and collaboration; service delivery; professional identity; organisational environment and wellbeing. Deductive mapping revealed that barriers and enablers were concentrated within three TDF domains: environmental context and resources, social influences, and skills, accounting for approximately 80% of all relevant determinants. This study offers critical insights into contextual and behavioural determinants influencing workforce practices in regional public oral healthcare. Addressing these challenges requires integrated strategies that strengthen professional identity, enhance culturally safe communication, expand workforce roles and leverage digital initiatives. Future research should focus on co-designing and evaluating theory-informed strategies to improve public oral healthcare services in regional settings.

title: Beyond Citations: A Dual-Dimension Evaluation Framework for Assessing Cognitive and Practical Competence of Top Scholars
abstract: Traditional bibliometric indicators such as the H-index and C-score often fail to distinguish theoretical researchers, practical engineers and speculative scholars effectively. To resolve this evaluation defect, this study revises the definitions of the cognitive index (HC) and practical index (HP). On this basis, a dual-index model incorporating information-theoretic weighting method and linear weighting is constructed to derive the comprehensive competence index (HE). Different from conventional single-dimensional evaluation systems that rely heavily on citations, the HE index emphasizes both cognitive level and practical performance when measuring academic talents. This study conducts empirical analysis on representative scientists ranked within the top 20 of the C-score list including Geoffrey Hinton, and the results prove that the proposed HE index can well reflect the actual industrial influence of researchers. Simulation tests covering educational stages from high school to doctoral level further verify the stability and validity of the model. In the digital era, academic evaluation increasingly requires practical value orientation. The HE evaluation framework provides a more reasonable and comprehensive way to assess the overall capacity of scientific and engineering talents. The HE index represents a paradigm shift: it posits that talent evaluation must prioritize a dual assessment of cognition and practice over a singular reliance on academic citations.

title: Reconceptualising LLM-Mediated Writing Practices: A Relational Approach
abstract: Since the launch of ChatGPT in 2022, large language models (LLMs) have fundamentally transformed writing practices across various domains. This study reconceptualises human–LLM writing through Michel Serres’s relational epistemology, considering LLMs as ‘quasi-objects’ that mediate collective knowledge production. For this purpose, it begins by proposing two complementary metaphors of relational writing: knotting and parasitising. Knotting demonstrates how LLMs serve as connective quasi-objects that synthesise disparate domains into hybrid knowledge spaces, while parasitising captures the capacity of LLMs to introduce productive interference that disrupts established writing patterns, creating novel meaning- making pathways through asymmetrical exchange. Then, through an analysis of academic writing, online content generation, creative storytelling and an educational experiment, I discuss how the relational dynamics of knotting and parasitising transform the ethical concerns of authorship, diversity, creativity and authenticity, as matters of relational responsibility. This study concludes by advocating for LLM literacy that emphasises contextual sensitivity to human–artificial intelligence (AI) configurations, richer discursive figurations and posthuman pedagogical approaches.

title: The Cost of Gold: Commodity Shocks and Violence Against Peasants in Brazil
authors: Bruno Pantaleão, Daniel Rio Tinto, Sofia Antonelli Amaral
abstract: Commodity booms can generate economic gains and social costs at once, particularly where institutions are weak. This paper provides evidence that gold-price shocks raise violence and agrarian conflict in the Brazilian Amazon, and that they do so by expanding gold extraction itself. We build a shift-share (Bartik) design---interpretable as a continuous difference-in-differences---that combines pre-sample municipal exposure to mining with international gold prices and exchange-rate variation, together with satellite-based measures of mining activity. Exogenous increases in gold profitability expand artisanal (garimpo) and overall gold mining, and this expansion raises homicide rates, the number of agrarian conflicts, and the number of peasant families in conflict, with the largest and most precisely estimated homicide effects borne by Black and mixed-race populations. The effects are concentrated in municipalities with exploitable gold endowments and differ across extraction regimes: garimpo mining maps onto lethal violence, while broader gold-mining activity maps onto land conflict. We find no comparable effects for industrial extraction, and evidence that official homicide counts understate the true mortality burden of mining expansion. The findings challenge the view that formalization and property rights alone can mitigate the social costs of extractive booms, and point to entrenched criminal landscapes that benefit from state inaction.

title: Transport-induced gentrification, perceived accessibility, and mobility practices: What happens to those who stay in their communities?
authors: Rebeca Froés de Assis, Carlos Felipe Grangeiro Loureiro, Ignacio Tiznado-Aitken
abstract: Transport-induced gentrification (TIG) has received growing scholarly attention, often focusing on displaced groups. Much less is known about the experiences of low-income incumbents who remain in place, particularly in unequal cities as the ones in the Global South. This paper addresses this gap, investigating if TIG generates reliance on motorized modes due to the social exclusion of low-income incumbents. We argue that urban reshaping near newly implemented transport infrastructures and services may isolate low-income communities as nearby opportunities become less affordable, prompting incumbents to adapt their mobility practices, reshaping where and how they travel to engage in out-of-home activities. Using perceived accessibility as an analytical lens, we applied a qualitative approach named process tracing method in an informal settlement impacted by a Light Rail Transit system in Fortaleza, Brazil. Evidence was collected through 29 semi-structured interviews and assessed using process tracing tests. Our findings suggest that while incumbents benefit from the proximity to new supermarkets, they feel increasingly excluded from nearby leisure and commercial opportunities. Consequently, many rely on motorized modes and on-demand transport services to reach more distant destinations. These findings contribute to the transportation research agenda by framing TIG as a distinct type of Transport-related Social Exclusion and demonstrate how urban reshaping near transit stations may undermine perceived accessibility for low-income incumbents, even when objective accessibility appears to improve.

title: HIỆN TRẠNG VÀ TƯƠNG LAI HỆ SINH THÁI BÁN DẪN VIỆT NAM 2026: Phân tích các hoạt động xây dựng hệ sinh thái và vai trò của Nhà đầu tư then chốt
authors: Willem Smit, Tobias Burgers, Quan Le, Phuoc Sac Tran
abstract: Nghiên cứu này xem xét quá trình tiến hóa của hệ sinh thái bán dẫn Việt Nam trong giai đoạn 2006–2026 và đánh giá vai trò của các tập đoàn đa quốc gia lớn trong việc định hình sự phát triển của hệ sinh thái này. Dựa trên khung lý thuyết hệ sinh thái, lý thuyết cụm ngành và góc nhìn chuỗi giá trị toàn cầu (GVC), báo cáo này phân tích cách thức các doanh nghiệp, cơ quan hoạch định chính sách, và cơ sở giáo dục - đào tạo đã cộng hưởng nguồn lực để đưa Việt Nam trở thành chủ thể quan trọng trong ngành công nghiệp bán dẫn thế giới. Thông qua việc khai thác các văn bản chính sách, dữ liệu ngành, tài liệu học thuật, hồ sơ doanh nghiệp và phỏng vấn chuyên sâu các bên liên quan, nghiên cứu đánh giá toàn diện bảy hoạt động kiến tạo hệ sinh thái: phát triển chính sách, thu hút đầu tư, kiến tạo hạ tầng, nâng cao năng lực đổi mới sáng tạo, chuyển đổi lực lượng lao động, nâng cấp nhà cung ứng và kết nối chuỗi giá trị toàn cầu. Kết quả nghiên cứu chỉ ra rằng Việt Nam đã có bước chuyển mình mạnh mẽ từ hoạt động lắp ráp điện tử đơn thuần trở thành nơi sản xuất bán dẫn có quy mô phức tạp công nghệ cao, đặc biệt là ở phân khúc lắp ráp, kiểm thử và đóng gói (ATP). Tiến trình chuyển đổi này tăng tốc vượt bậc từ năm 2018 nhờ sự tác động tổng thể từ những biến chuyển địa chính trị, xu hướng đa dạng hóa chuỗi cung ứng, các chính sách công nghiệp có chủ đích và dòng vốn đầu tư bền bỉ từ các tập đoàn đa quốc gia. Nghiên cứu sử dụng trường hợp (case study) của Intel Products Vietnam (IPV) để minh chứng cách thức một nhà đầu tư trực tiếp nước ngoài (FDI) thực hiện nhiều vai trò của một Nhà đầu tư then chốt (Keystone Investor) từ 20 năm trước. Theo đó, tầm ảnh hưởng của IPV đã lan tỏa vượt ngoài phạm vi đóng góp kinh tế trực tiếp, thông qua việc xây dựng nguồn nhân lực, nâng cao năng lực thể chế, nâng cấp mạng lưới nhà cung ứng, tham vấn chính sách và điều phối hệ sinh thái. Báo cáo đề xuất một khung chiến lược để hiểu rõ hơn cách thức các nhà đầu tư tiên phong đóng góp vào việc phát triển hệ sinh thái tại các nền kinh tế mới nổi, đồng thời nhận diện lộ trình chiến lược tiếp theo để Việt Nam bứt phá từ vị thế tập trung vào ATP đơn thuần sang các hoạt động có mức độ đổi mới sáng tạo và giá trị gia tăng cao hơn. Các hoạt động này bao gồm: đóng gói tiên tiến, thiết kế vi mạch, nghiên cứu và phát triển (R&D) định hướng ứng dụng và sản xuất công nghệ cao. Những phát hiện nghiên cứu sẽ đóng góp thiết thực vào các thảo luận về chiến lược nâng cấp công nghiệp, phát triển hệ sinh thái tự cường và vai trò của các doanh nghiệp đa quốc gia trong việc hình thành năng lực công nghệ nội địa.

title: Die Zukunft der Sexarbeit. Rechtsmodelle, Plattformökonomie und Arbeitsorganisation im internationalen Vergleich. Eine theoretische Synthese.
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work along three interlocking axes: legal governance models, platformisation, and labour organisation. Drawing exclusively on peer-reviewed research and methodologically transparent evaluations - including the systematic review by Platt et al. (2018), the Lancet series on HIV and sex work (Shannon et al. 2015), the New Zealand evaluation literature following the Prostitution Reform Act 2003, the German federal evaluation of the Prostituiertenschutzgesetz (KFN 2025), and emerging implementation research on Belgium's 2024 labour law framework - it argues that the future of sex work will be decided less in criminal law than in labour law and digital infrastructure. The paper identifies a structural paradox: while the epidemiological and criminological evidence increasingly supports decriminalisation and labour rights, the political momentum in Europe, reinforced by the European Parliament's 2023 resolution and the European Court of Human Rights' 2024 ruling on the French client criminalisation law, points in the opposite direction. Simultaneously, platform capitalism and financial infrastructure exercise a form of private, extraterritorial regulation - exemplified by FOSTA-SESTA's global chilling effects - that increasingly overrides national legal frameworks. Three scenarios for 2040 are developed: labour-law normalisation (the Belgian path), neo-abolitionist expansion, and platform-mediated informalisation. The paper concludes that the third scenario is currently the most probable default, and that only deliberate institutional design - of the kind pioneered in Belgium - can shift the trajectory.

title: The Geopolitics of Cryptography: End-to-End Encryption at the Intersection of Technology, Security, and Sovereignty
abstract: End-to-end encryption (E2EE) has become a central site of conflict among privacy rights, national security, and digital sovereignty. This paper integrates comparative policy analysis, technical feasibility assessment, and multi-stakeholder analysis to examine the international dynamics of E2EE regulation across twenty jurisdictions. It develops an original five-part typology—prohibitionist, conditional-access, permissive, hybrid, and network-shutdown-dependent—and argues that regulatory divergence is shaped less by technical constraints than by how digital sovereignty is conceptualized, the character of political regimes, and institutional linkages with the lawful-interception industry. The paper further demonstrates that backdoor demands have persisted for thirty years despite their technical infeasibility. This persistence is explained by a self-reinforcing cycle in which organizational reputation maintenance, security-industry interests, and political framing around child protection and counterterrorism mutually reinforce each other. Platform companies are repositioned as quasi-sovereign actors whose norm-making capacity over encrypted digital communications rivals that of states. These private entities exercise strategic resistance through technical architecture and cryptographic discourse, doing so selectively as a function of market scale and state coercive capacity. The paper incorporates events through May 2026, including the January 2025 UK Technical Capability Notice to Apple, the subsequent US-brokered withdrawal of that demand, and the April 2026 expiry of the EU CSAR 1.0 interim scanning regime. From this analysis it derives four principles of regulatory design: prior technical feasibility assessment, proportionality, international interoperability, and multi-stakeholder governance inclusive of the Global South.

title: DEFENSIVE DECISION ARCHITECTURE: EXPLAINING MANAGERIAL CHOICE UNDER CONDITIONS OF AUDIT INTENSITY
abstract: This study develops a theory-building framework explaining how audit intensity reshapes managerial decision-making in high-scrutiny organizational environments. Existing research commonly attributes conservative managerial behavior to risk aversion, compliance culture, blame avoidance, or organizational capacity limitations. While these perspectives provide important insights, they do not adequately explain how sustained evaluative scrutiny becomes translated into a stable decision logic that systematically influences managerial choice. Drawing on audit culture theory, prospect theory, and behavioral decision research, the study introduces the concept of Defensive Decision Architecture (DDA). DDA refers to the systematic reordering of managerial decision criteria under conditions of sustained audit intensity, whereby procedural defensibility, audit resilience, and exposure minimization become prioritized over substantive optimization, innovation, and adaptive performance. Unlike related concepts such as risk aversion, blame avoidance, and procedural compliance, DDA emphasizes the institutional restructuring of decision criteria rather than isolated behavioral responses. The framework proposes that audit intensity functions not merely as an external constraint but as an institutional condition that reshapes how managers evaluate alternatives and define acceptable risk. As defensibility becomes increasingly salient, behaviors such as documentation expansion, procedural standardization, discretion avoidance, and reliance on precedent emerge as rational adaptations to accountability environments characterized by continuous scrutiny. The study contributes to organizational decision theory by providing a mechanism-based explanation of conservative managerial behavior and offers analytically derived propositions to guide future empirical research on audit intensity, accountability, and organizational decision-making.

title: Does Affective Intelligence Theory Generalize? Evidence from the Russia-Ukraine War
abstract: During international crises, domestic populations encounter competing strategic narratives that shape public understanding. Affective Intelligence Theory (AIT) suggests that anxiety disrupts habitual political thinking, prompting individuals to seek more information, engage with a broader range of sources, and exhibit greater openness to counter-attitudinal perspectives. While well-supported in Western democratic contexts, its generalizability remains underexplored. This study tests AIT during an international crisis in Kazakhstan, where residents confront competing narratives about Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine. I field a preregistered online experiment (n = 649) with a validated anxiety induction and measure information behavior. The results show no significant effects of anxiety on these behaviors, challenging AIT’s assumptions. Despite successful anxiety induction, respondents did not seek more information, engage with diverse media, or reconsider prior attitudes. These findings suggest that the predictions of AIT are conditional, highlighting the need to revisit how emotional mechanisms shape information behavior across diverse information environments.

title: Uso de inteligencia artificial generativa en educación matemática: revisión sistemática de aplicaciones didácticas, beneficios, riesgos y desafíos para la práctica docente
abstract: Este artículo revisa de manera sistemática la evidencia empírica disponible sobre el uso de inteligencia artificial generativa en educación matemática, con especial atención a la práctica docente en educación secundaria y a los estudios de formación de profesores con transferencia directa a la matemática escolar. Siguiendo los lineamientos de reporte de PRISMA 2020, se realizó una búsqueda estructurada en repositorios académicos y portales editoriales accesibles el 7 de abril de 2026, con ventana temporal desde noviembre de 2022 hasta marzo de 2026. El corpus final quedó integrado por 16 estudios empíricos, apreciados mediante una matriz adaptada del MMAT con cuatro criterios de calidad; adicionalmente, se emplearon revisiones recientes y documentos de política educativa para contextualizar la discusión. La síntesis identifica cuatro usos predominantes: apoyo tutorial y resolución de problemas, generación y rediseño de tareas y planes de clase, simulaciones para la formación docente, y estudios de validación de la calidad matemática del output generado por modelos conversacionales. Los beneficios reportados se concentran en la retroalimentación inmediata, la diferenciación didáctica, la eficiencia en la preparación docente y la apertura de espacios para el análisis de errores y la metacognición. Los riesgos más consistentes son la inexactitud matemática, la sobredependencia del alumnado, la fragilidad de las respuestas en geometría, cálculo y pruebas, y la insuficiencia de políticas escolares sobre privacidad, integridad académica y alfabetización en IA. Como contribución interpretativa, se propone el constructo de Juicio Didáctico-Matemático sobre el Output de IA (JDMO) como competencia profesional nuclear para la integración responsable de la IA generativa en matemáticas escolares. Se concluye que la IA generativa resulta más prometedora como herramienta mediada por el profesor que como tutor autónomo, y que su integración más robusta aparece cuando se combina con verificación matemática, rediseño de la evaluación y software disciplinar.

title: Enclave City: How the Hinterland’s Comparative Advantage Shapes Its Fate
authors: Mahastri Laksita Sam Widita, Raisal Ahmad
abstract: The morphological premise of Indonesia’s administrative enclave cities, that a flat city at the geographic center of a regency will function as a service node for the producing hinterland around it, is collapsing functionally, even while the administrative and geographic structures remain intact. This paper compares two Indonesian enclave cities administratively enclosed by their regencies (Solok City in West Sumatra and Mojokerto City in East Java), using Location Quotient, Shift-Share analysis, Klassen Typology, Kernel Density Estimation, and cross-regional correlation matrices of sectoral competitiveness structures across three COVID-19 pandemic eras. Two structurally distinct fates are identified: in Solok, the regency is gradually building competing service capacity that erodes the city’s role (administrative-relic enclave); in Mojokerto, the regency bypasses the city to connect directly with Surabaya’s metropolitan industry, while the city survives through high-tertiary sector specialization (disintegrate enclave). The two fates diverge on a single variable: the comparative advantage of the surrounding regency. The paper proposes administrative-specialist enclavement as a third form of urban enclosure beyond the exclusion (Kleibert 2018) and zoning technology (Ong 2004) concepts that currently dominate enclave discourse. Keywords: enclave city, urban-rural linkage, central place theory, comparative advantage, Solok, Mojokerto.

title: Urban Cannibalism: Expanding Agglomeration Shadow to Function Extraction in Arrested Transition
authors: Mahastri Laksita Sam Widita, Raisal Ahmad
abstract: Indonesia’s metropolitan connectivity infrastructure, which is premised on equitable development, operates in reverse. In Gerbangkertosusila, the regency closest to Surabaya grew the slowest, as seen in Bangkalan after the opening of the Suramadu Bridge. The agglomeration shadow literature (Meijers and Burger 2010) explains why metropolitan peripheries grow slowly but does not distinguish transitional shadows from structural, extractive ones. Friedmann’s (1966) spatial evolution model assumes a phase transition that, in this case, has stalled. This paper introduces the concept of cannibalism to name the active-extractive mode of agglomeration shadow that emerges when a metropolitan system is trapped in arrested transition, and operationalizes it through the Cannibalism Index, the ratio of external pull to local strength, computed across 149 H3 hexagons with a Newton–Reilly gravity model built on OpenStreetMap Point of Interest data and O’Sullivan’s urbanization economies framework, then supplemented by Location Quotient and Shift-Share analyses of sectoral GRDP. Results show seven Agglomeration Core hexagons, all concentrated in Surabaya City; no Independent Hub emerges outside the core, and sixteen Shadow Zone hexagons mark areas vulnerable to cannibalization. A three-way distance-decay test on Bangkalan, Mojokerto, and Lamongan finds that distance protects peripheries from core pull but does not build internal functional strength. The Mojokerto case study reveals a function-extractive split: industry settles in the regency through Ngoro Industrial Park, while the professional services that should accompany it remain in Surabaya. Gerbangkertosusila is more accurately classified as a polynuclear monocentric region in arrested transition than a mature polycentric system. This diagnosis carries direct planning implications: connectivity without functional deconcentration is not a strategy for empowering peripheries but a state-facilitated extraction mechanism. Keywords: agglomeration shadow, urban cannibalism, arrested transition, functional polycentricity, cannibalism index, Gerbangkertosusila.

title: العمل الحزبي بالمغرب بين أزمة التنظيم و غياب الفاعلية
abstract: كما يمكن القول أن أزمة الأحزاب السياسية قد أدت إلى افشال أو في طريقها الى افشال تحقيق تحول ديمقراطي، خصوصا بعد الأمل الذي بني عليها ما بعد دستور 2011 و تحقيق المطالب التي جاء بها الشارع ابان الحراك الديمقراطي و نجاح المغرب في امتصاص غضب الشارع عن طريق تقديم مجموعة من الإصلاحات التي كانت تعتبر أنذاك مشعل أمل. فتم منح صلاحيات واسعة للأحزاب أهمها تكوين حكومات بصلاحيات أوسع مع نضيرتها السابقة، إلا أن الممارسة لحد الساعة تتنبأ بفشل التحول الديمقراطي .فهل يمكن الحديث عن إعادة هيكلة الأحزاب من جديد لتتوافق و المرحلة الجديدة أم ان الحل يكمن في تبني مقاربات قانونية و سياسية لتجاوز الأزمة. فربما حان الوقت للتنضيمات الحزبية إلى فتح الباب امام النخب الشابة لعلها تكون الحاملة لمشعل تجديد الخطاب الحزبي و تأدية الواجب الحزبي المنصوص عليه الدستور و عدم تقزيم دور الحزب السياسي في المشاركة الانتخابية لا غير و تغيب عن المجتمع و عن الوطن.

title: Социальные сети как инфраструктура продаж для МСП: экономическая оценка влияния контент-стратегий и рекламных форматов на конверсию и выручку (Social Networks as Sales Infrastructure for SMEs: An Economic Assessment of the Impact of Content Strategies and Advertising Formats on Conversion and Revenue)
abstract: Abstract. The purpose of this study is to comprehensively assess the economic impact of various content strategies and social media advertising formats on conversion rates and revenue for small and medium-sized businesses. Key findings indicate that integrating educational content with direct sales increases conversion rates by 34-47%, while using video formats increases the average order value by 28%. The optimal ratio of organic to paid traffic for SMEs was found to be 60:40. The author's findings confirm the need for a systematic approach to developing a content strategy that takes into account the specifics of the target audience and the characteristics of the product category. Keywords: social media, small and medium businesses, content strategy, conversion, digital marketing, cost-effectiveness, SMM, sales.

title: Empirical Verification of an Asset Dynamics Model in Open Economies: An Equilibrium Theory Endogenizing Imbalances Bridging Steady-State Conditions and Social Adaptation
abstract: The general equilibrium theory, upon which mainstream modern macroeconomics relies, fails to adequately explain the realities enveloping the contemporary economy, including persistent global imbalances. In contrast, our previous studies (Kitamura, 2025, 2026) have proposed a theoretical model based on "asset dynamics" that conceptualizes the global economy as a dissipative structure woven by the concentration and diffusion of funds. We understand it as an "equilibrium theory endogenizing imbalances" where economies, each harboring its own imbalances, maintain a balance by compensating for each other's surpluses and deficits. This paper statistically verifies the empirical validity of this theoretical framework using multi-source macroeconomic data and socio-psychological indicators. Targeting approximately 80 countries for which data are available, we redefine a socio-psychological indicator based on a large-scale international survey regarding time value, patience, and other attributes, as a proxy for the divergence between the real interest rate and the time preference rate—which constitutes one side of the steady-state condition derived from our theoretical model. Conducting a regression analysis using the structural terms that constitute the right-hand side of the conditional equation, namely population changes, capital transfer, and asset preference, we obtained statistically significant results, including the sign conditions. Furthermore, by utilizing these estimation results, we visualized the dissipative structure that weaves the global economy, rendering the contrasting positions and interdependence of the two major economic superpowers as data-driven facts, such as the United States as a self-organizing economy and China as a dissipative economy. The contributions of this paper provide an empirical foundation for a new macroeconomic paradigm that reframes imbalances not as an undesirable state to be eliminated, but endogenously explains them as the energy required for the global economic system to maintain its vitality.

title: Visible Threats, Invisible Lives: A Constructivist Grounded Theory of Non-Heteronormative Visibility Management in Qazaqstan
authors: Dinara Pisareva, Nurmakhan Tastaibek, Aruzhan Auyez, Aruzhan Kaparova, Diana Molzhigit, Sultan Satybaldy, Nikita Durnev, Maiya Talipova
abstract: In 2025, Qazaqstan passed a law banning LGBT “propaganda.” Among other things, it raised the question of how Qazaqstanis construct meaning around issues related to sexual and gender minorities (SGM). Our paper answers this question through a constructivist grounded theory analysis of 808 survey responses and six follow-up interviews. We find that in Qazaqstan the boundary between tolerating and opposing queer people is often drawn at how visible they are. Our results show that ordinary people are as concerned with non-heteronormative visibility management as queer people are. We identify three meaning-making frameworks that respondents use when engaging with questions about sexual and gender minorities: a visibility-contingent framework (77%), a principled-acceptance framework (14%), and an existence-rejection framework (6%). Respondents in the visibility-contingent framework make sense of SGM by deliberating how much visibility is allowed and on which terms. We also show that language strongly shapes the discursive repertoire of our respondents. Qazaq speakers express more negative attitudes (71% vs. 37%) and often see queer visibility as a threat, while Russian speakers talk about private sexuality and conditional tolerance. Overall, these findings assist in explaining popular support for the propaganda law, whose non-heteronormative visibility logic mirrors our respondents’ own.

title: Sociological and Economic Factors Shaping the Culture of Spectacle in the United States, in Comparison with Poland and Europe
abstract: The study offers a multidimensional account of the culture of spectacle in the United States, Poland and Europe, examined through the sociology of popular music, the economics of mass events, media, politics, sport, advertising, sponsorship and audience expectations. It discusses major American mega-events such as the Super Bowl Halftime Show, the Grammy Awards and Coachella, alongside European and Polish models of cultural organization, funding, infrastructure and event production. The work shows how contemporary spectacle culture is shaped by a complex interplay of market, social, media-related and institutional factors.

title: Who Governs Cyberspace in the age of AI? A Global Stocktake of Cybersecurity Governance
abstract: Cyberspace is not ungoverned—it is systematically misgoverned. International instruments, national strategies, and voluntary frameworks have proliferated while security outcomes deteriorated. This inversion—more governance, less resilience—is the "Proliferation Paradox." The paper introduces "Governance Debt": the institutional deficit that accumulates when AI-driven technological change outpaces governance adaptation. This debt compounds across three dimensions: Capacity Debt (threat velocity exceeds institutional response cycles), Authority Debt (no enforcement jurisdiction over borderless AI infrastructure), and Legitimacy Debt (governance authority migrates to unaccountable private actors). This paper examines cybersecurity governance in 29 countries. It identifies three institutional models: Integrated Command, Nodal Hybrid, and Fragmented Sectoral. Key finding: institutional architecture predicts operational resilience more consistently than strategy quality or financial investment. The analysis covers 66 governance instruments and reveals four critical weaknesses in how AI challenges current systems: Scale (velocity), Attribution (identity), Dependence (vulnerability to foreign platforms), and Agency (autonomous systems without accountability). The paper calls cloud providers, AI developers, and standards bodies "private leviathans": they exercise sovereign-scale power over cyberspace without democratic mandate or meaningful accountability. Using the OECD Strategic Foresight Toolkit, the paper stress-tests six governance models across four 2030 scenarios. Two extremes fail: total centralization (a Global Cyber Agency) and total market-led governance. The "Adaptive Federated Stack"—a five-layer architecture—distributes authority across existing institutions without creating a new apex body. This model performs across all scenarios and resolves all three Governance Debt dimensions. The central argument: between 2026 and 2030, governance success requires interoperability, not unity.

title: Affective Political Communication and Voters' Choice of Candidates during Elections
abstract: Political campaigns in modern day democracies have increasingly relied on emotional appeals, symbolic messaging and identity- based narratives to mobilise voters and shape public opinion. This study examined affective political communication and voters' choices of candidates during elections. Affective political communication involves the strategic use of emotions such as fear, hope, anger and enthusiasm to influence political attitudes and behaviours. In many political settings, political actors deploy various communication channels including campaign rallies, traditional media and social media platforms to disseminate emotionally framed messages aimed at persuading voters. The researcher adopted the library research method for the study, while the theoretical framework was hinged on Affective Intelligence Theory. Findings from the study revealed that affective political communication plays a significant role in voters' choice of candidates during elections. Also, emotional appeals embedded in campaign messages influences how voters interpret the personality, credibility and leadership qualities of candidates. Further findings revealed that media exposure particularly through social media platforms and political campaign events amplifies affective messages and contribute to the formation of positive or negative voter attitudes toward candidates. From the findings, the researcher recommended among other things, that political actors and campaign organisations adopt responsible and issue- based communication strategies that emphasise credible information rather than manipulative emotional appeals.

title: Monetary-Fiscal Nexus and Price Level Dynamics in Ghana: An ARDL Analysis of Public Debt Servicing, Short-Term Debt, and Inflation (1980-2020)
abstract: This study investigates the macroeconomic consequences of public debt composition and servicing obligations on price stability in Ghana from 1980 to 2020. Operating within a dual-model framework, the empirical analysis distinguishes between the cash-flow effects of public debt servicing (PDS) and the maturity-risk impacts of short-term public debt (SPD). The econometric methodology employs Dickey-Fuller Generalised Least Squares (DF-GLS) and Phillips-Perron unit root tests alongside an Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) bounds testing approach to accommodate mixed integration orders among the variables. The bounds test confirms a stable, long-run cointegrating relationship in both the PDS and SPD specifications. Empirically, the study reveals that PDS exerts a statistically significant negative effect on domestic inflation in both the short run (coefficient = -0.81, p < 0.10) and the long run (coefficient = -0.93, p < 0.05). Conversely, SPD remains statistically insignificant across both temporal horizons. These findings challenge the conventional predictions of the fiscal theory of the price level, suggesting instead that heavy debt-servicing requirements contract aggregate demand by crowding out productive public expenditure. Among the control variables, exchange rate depreciation acts as the dominant positive driver of price levels, while economic growth serves as a robust dampening factor. The error correction terms (-0.87 and -0.90) indicate a rapid speed of adjustment toward long-run equilibrium. These findings suggest that debt restructuring should be coupled with careful demand management, and emphasize that stabilizing local currency value should be prioritized over adjusting debt maturity profiles to maintain overall economic stability.

title: Demand estimation and pricing of perishable food
abstract: Demand estimation of perishable food products is challenging because freshness is typically not recorded in available data. To address this measurement challenge, I collect novel customer-transaction and batch-level-production panels—enabling direct observation of freshness for each individual unit in both consumer purchases and choice sets—from the prepared fried-food category of a Japanese grocery chain. With this unique data, I study consumer willingness-to-pay for freshness, its joint heterogeneous distribution with price sensitivity, and grocers’ optimal pricing by freshness using improved demand estimation. I build and estimate a model of consumer batch choice (i.e., freshness) for perishable goods, capturing heterogeneous freshness preferences and price sensitivity. I find that consumer demand for freshness is substantial, with an additional hour of freshness for an average product equivalent to 4.0% of its full price, but freshness preferences also correlate with price sensitivity, implying an absence of purely price-driven consumers. I then model the retailer’s dynamic markdown problem—trading-off discount losses against future stockout losses—and show that optimal markdowns can be zero under observed heterogeneity and the production process. I implement the proposed policy in the focal store; the structural model predicts a focal profit increase of 2.24% (95% posterior predictive interval [1.91, 2.60]), and a field implementation yields a profit increase of about 6.3%, consistent with this prediction. My findings can explain cross-grocer differences in pricing-by-freshness policies as a function of consumer heterogeneity.

title: The Future of Sex Work Legal Models, Platform Economy, and Labour Organisation in International Comparison. A Theoretical Synthesis.
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work along three interlocking axes: legal governance models, platformisation, and labour organisation. Drawing exclusively on peer-reviewed research and methodologically transparent evaluations - including the systematic review by Platt et al. (2018), the Lancet series on HIV and sex work (Shannon et al. 2015), the New Zealand evaluation literature following the Prostitution Reform Act 2003, the German federal evaluation of the Prostituiertenschutzgesetz (KFN 2025), and emerging implementation research on Belgium's 2024 labour law framework - it argues that the future of sex work will be decided less in criminal law than in labour law and digital infrastructure. The paper identifies a structural paradox: while the epidemiological and criminological evidence increasingly supports decriminalisation and labour rights, the political momentum in Europe, reinforced by the European Parliament's 2023 resolution and the European Court of Human Rights' 2024 ruling on the French client criminalisation law, points in the opposite direction. Simultaneously, platform capitalism and financial infrastructure exercise a form of private, extraterritorial regulation - exemplified by FOSTA-SESTA's global chilling effects - that increasingly overrides national legal frameworks. Three scenarios for 2040 are developed: labour-law normalisation (the Belgian path), neo-abolitionist expansion, and platform-mediated informalisation. The paper concludes that the third scenario is currently the most probable default, and that only deliberate institutional design - of the kind pioneered in Belgium - can shift the trajectory. Canonical German version https://osf.io/preprints/socarxiv/df43j_v1

title: Pensamiento Periférico y Poder Periférico. Una reflexión conjunta necesaria.
authors: Andrés Miguel Figueroa Jiménez. Dr. c of American Studies
abstract: E. Devés en su teoría Pensamiento Periférico plantea una tesis interpretativa global para entender, desde las arquitecturas y despliegues de ideas a los desarrollos multinivel de agentes periféricos en el pasado, en el presente y también para dar claridad a los intentos societales de estos agentes por construir horizontes de futuro. El núcleo analítico de Devés consiste en el siguiente razonamiento empíricamente sostenido: “La manera más comprensiva de entender el pensamiento emergido en las regiones periféricas durante los últimos siglos –regiones en general sometidas a procesos de invasión o colonización– consiste en la disyuntiva: ser-como-el-centro versus ser-nosotros-mismos. Este problema se lo ha planteado la intelectualidad latinoamericana, la africana y la asiática, como también la eslava, la balcánica y la ibérica de esta época”. No obstante, en su desarrollo, la propuesta de Devés muestra, a nuestro juicio, un flanco abierto, una “Grieta analítica” que impactaría en su performance como insumo explicativo e interpretativo. Este flanco abierto es la débil o casi inexistente incorporación a la teoría de Devés de una reflexión sobre el poder y particularmente respecto del poder de los agentes que su obra pretende sacar a flote, a saber: los agentes periféricos, débiles y justamente los más sensibles a los efectos de formulaciones y acciones de poder. Ante la problemática expuesta, se presenta como alternativa para una colaboración analítica, para una reflexión conjunta, la categoría: Poder Periférico. Lo anterior, a través de la explicitación de sus componentes (principios justificativos, características fundamentales y condiciones generales), sus dimensiones y sus intensidades. Para este ejercicio son elegidos con fines solamente ilustrativos 5 gobiernos de Chile entre (1952-1990). En esta operación disciplinar coordinada, se provocan avances hacia el cierre o disminución de la grieta analítica y se articula el inicio de una comunión entre la teoría Pensamiento Periférico y el concepto de Poder Periférico que fortalece al desarrollo de tareas explicativas y comprensivas y sus resultados respecto a la realidad de agentes débiles y de los espacios periféricos. Palabras claves: A. Figueroa, E. Devés, Grieta analítica, Pensamiento Periférico, Poder Periférico.

title: Beyond Augmentation: Epistemic Complementarity in LLM-Assisted Qualitative Analysis
authors: Alina Ionela Palimaru, Ryan Brown, Daniel Siconolfi, Michael S. Dunbar, Nipher Malika, Prateek Puri, Elizabeth J. D’Amico
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly entering qualitative analysis workflows, yet the field's discussion of their integration has unfolded as though "qualitative research" were a single tradition with a single epistemological stake. Drawing on a review of fifty-one studies on LLM-assisted qualitative analysis published between 2024 and 2026, we observe that approximately 80% do not articulate an epistemological stance, while those that do rarely work within their stated paradigm as a constraint on methodological decisions. The result is a debate that conflates important issues: interpretivist scholars mount forceful objections to LLM use that may not extend to applied qualitative work, while pragmatic integrators describe sophisticated workflows without consistently operationalizing the standards by which their tradition assesses analytic adequacy. In this article, we argue that the integration question is methodology-specific and must be answered on the terms each tradition sets for itself. We then offer a worked answer for one pathway through the qualitative research landscape: a pragmatic narrative inquiry combining Framework Analysis with Codebook Thematic Analysis, drawn from the CHAT project, a PCORI-funded study of adolescent substance use involving 86 semi-structured interviews and 5,466 coded excerpts analyzed through Muse QDA, a purpose-built LLM-assisted qualitative analysis platform. Our framework rests on three principles: epistemic capacity, which specifies what each agent (human, LLM) is positioned to claim at each phase of analysis; analytic significance, which distinguishes calibration interventions (instrument-tuning) from meaning-making interventions (analysis-shaping) and locates the latter at tradition-specific decision points; and trajectory documentation, which preserves the path of analytic judgment without claiming reproducibility of outputs. The framework's distinctive theoretical contribution is a strict form of complementarity: human and LLM epistemic capacities are non-substitutable within this workflow, such that neither agent can do the other's work without epistemic loss. We illustrate the framework through a three-track codebook development process, a documented case of conceptual sharpening between two parent codes, and a "What did I miss?" exercise in which LLM coverage prompted human meaning-making. We close by considering the framework's limits, including resource demands, tool dependence, and the question of who gets to do LLM-assisted qualitative research, and by arguing that QDA platform design in the LLM era is itself a methodological matter. The integration question is not whether LLMs can do qualitative research, but for which tradition, under which paradigmatic commitments, do which kinds of integration satisfy that tradition's own standards.

title: Information Limits in Quantitative Research
abstract: When is a research design as informative as it can plausibly be for a given research setting? Social science commonly features small samples, confounders, sparse treatments, and other limitations to precision. Conventional power analyses evaluate designs against context-agnostic asymptotics, ignoring the empirical limits of applied research settings. We develop a framework for analyzing the informational efficiency of a constrained research design against an adaptive benchmark. By defining a theoretical limit of recoverable data and evaluating its divergence from observed data, researchers can formally quantify the relative precision and stability of their estimates within the scope of their research design. Simulations and applications demonstrate that increasing sample size does not always improve precision. Our framework offers a principled means of identifying the scope of additional obtainable data, the extent of uncertainty attributable to this unrealized, but feasible data collection, and the contexts in which a study has reached its information limit.

title: Inequitable Aftermath: Structural Mechanisms Shaping the Experience of Firearm Violence
abstract: Background. The aftermath of firearm violence is shaped by the same structural conditions that determine exposure, yet their role in producing differential post-exposure experiences remains underspecified. This study identifies the mechanisms through which these conditions generate inequitable aftermath experiences across four exposure typologies. Methods. Secondary qualitative analysis of 33 in-depth interviews with adults who had experienced firearm violence (physical injury, witnessing, bereavement, chronic community violence) in the United States. Guided by intersectionality as a sensitizing framework, analysis used iterative coding, cross-case matrices, and temporal analysis. Results. Seven mechanisms were identified: financial foreclosure, racialized institutional stigma, institutional recognition hierarchy, navigation burden, temporal abandonment, agency suppression, and compounding exposure. Mechanisms operated across rather than within exposure typologies, compounded one another systematically, and shaped identity from within while constraining material access. Outlier analysis specified the structural conditions under which each mechanism was suppressed. Intersectional analysis found that race and class operated both distinctly and in combination, producing qualitatively distinct burdens not reducible to either axis alone. Conclusions. The inequitable aftermath of firearm violence is structurally produced, not inevitable. The mechanisms identified provide a framework for intervention that targets institutional design failures, racialized processes, and temporally bounded support systems rather than individual-level coping. Intersectional analysis further demonstrates that race and class compound one another in ways that single-axis frameworks cannot capture, with direct implications for how interventions are designed and for whom.

title: Seeing Neglect: Measuring the Predictive Value of Public Aerial and Street-Level Imagery for Property Code Violations
abstract: We measure how well freely available imagery (county and USDA aerial orthophotos and Google Street View) predicts open residential code-enforcement violations, scoring hand-crafted color/texture features, zero-shot and aerial-finetuned CLIP, a DINOv2 probe, an open-vocabulary detector (OWLv2), and a small vision-language model (Qwen2-VL-2B) against matched same-neighborhood controls in Hillsborough County, Florida. We report four findings. First, a label-free Street View detector transfers across metros: with no retraining, pooled AUC in Broward County matches Hillsborough (0.58/0.59) and overgrowth replicates (0.63/0.64). Imagery is nearly universal while code-enforcement records are not, so a detector that transfers without local labels is the one that can actually be deployed. In a third metro with dated records (Jacksonville), the signal predates the record: pre-citation Street View predicts next-year first citations (overgrowth AUC 0.61, n=1,198) with significant lead up to 12 months, although it cannot predict citation timing among eventually-cited addresses. Second, model scale is not the lever: a four-number greenness statistic outperforms every deep aerial embedding, and a holistic neglect prompt outperforms targeted object detection.
