Third, cues are viewpoint-specific: overgrowth is detectable from the air (AUC 0.65) while debris is visible only from the street (0.50 vs 0.62), and the 0.6-0.7 AUC ceiling is consistent with a simple label-noise attenuation model. Fourth, the viewpoints are complementary: fusing them raises every signal, although the gains are not yet significant at n=352. We release an anonymized benchmark, code, and per-parcel features.

title: From ‘May Be Linked With’ to ‘Causes’: How Media and Academic Headlines Frame Causality
abstract: Media headlines are accused of exaggerating causal claims from scientific findings. We surveyed 55 authors of highly cited papers across eight fields (e.g., medicine). Participants rated the causal strength implied in real-world media headlines covering their work versus their article titles. The aggregate mean difference between media and authors was –0.08, indicating a tendency toward understatement. Approximately 28.3% of headlines overstated causality, while 33.2% understated it. The overall pattern among credible outlets was broadly neutral, contrasting with prior studies emphasizing systematic exaggeration. Misrepresentation of causality is heterogeneous, pointing to the need for clearer editorial standards to preserve scientific accuracy.

title: Ahead of the Firm: Cultural Lag, Employee Expectations, and the HR Predicament in Corporate Sustainability.
abstract: As corporate sustainability has migrated from the voluntary, ethics-based vocabulary of corporate social responsibility (CSR) toward the measured, disclosure-driven logic of ESG, human resource functions have been charged with aligning organizational culture to the ESG expectations of top management. This paper argues that the assignment is structurally constrained by two desynchronizations rather than by execution failure alone. First, the timescale on which organizational culture genuinely changes is, on average, longer than the tempo set by ESG's market and institutional pressure, producing a cultural lag that tempts firms toward symbolic conformity. Second, employee expectations, especially around the social pillar that ESG inherited from CSR, frequently run ahead of what firms believe they are doing and communicating, producing an expectation premium experienced, in daily working life, as a say–do gap. Drawing on organizational culture theory, the literature on temporal tensions and decoupling, and the micro-foundations of CSR, the paper reframes the HR predicament as the management of two unsynchronized clocks and an expectation gradient, and considers when employee anticipation is a liability and when it is a usable asset.

title: Who outsources domestic work in the Swedish welfare state? The role of absolute and relative resources
authors: Elien Dalman, Therese Nilsson, Annika Elwert, Christofer Edling, Lisa Eklund
abstract: Drawing on social stratification and relative resource theory, and gender ideology theory, this article investigates how class and gender shape outsourcing domestic work patterns within the Swedish welfare state, a forerunner in terms of gender equality. Using full-population longitudinal administrative registers from 2007 to 2022, we include individual-level information on domestic outsourcing following a generous tax deduction reform in 2007. The results reveal a strong class gradient in outsourcing, something which has shrunk markedly over time, suggesting a normative shift towards increased acceptance of domestic services. Regardless of absolute and relative resources, women outsource more than men. Multivariate analyses demonstrate outsourcing to be most common among (single and partnered) mothers, while childless singles rarely outsource domestic work. In couple-level analyses we demonstrate that outsourcing is not only shaped by absolute resources and time constraints, but also by gender ideology and relative resources. Without recognizing this intersection, analyses risk overstating the role of income while overlooking how relative resources and time constraint determine outsourcing. Specifically, results show that women who out-earn their partners outsource more, rather than renegotiating the within-couple division of unpaid labor. This suggests that outsourcing may be conducive to upholding the first phase of the gender revolution, by enabling women to pursue their high(er)-earning jobs, while hampering the second phase by removing incentives for men’s greater engagement in unpaid housework. By showing that couples from higher-class origins outsource more at a given income, we highlight how outsourcing may reinforce rather than reduce class as well as gender divides. The article further puts forward directions for future research.

title: Measuring Functional Access, Not Service Presence: The CORE Framework for Community Resource Deprivation with Context-Dependent Weights and Quantified Uncertainty
abstract: Widely used United States community-needs indices—including the Area Deprivation Index (ADI), the Social Vulnerability Index (SVI), the USDA Food Access Research Atlas (FARA), and federal shortage designations—generally measure population disadvantage, vulnerabil ity, shortage, or service proximity. They do not jointly measure whether a population can functionally use nearby services. This paper introduces CORE (Community Opportunity & Resource Engine), a ZCTA-level framework that scores functional access across ten essential resource pillars through four multiplicative determinants: Reachability by the travel mode residents actually use, Economic accessibility at actual disposable income, Availability at the times and capacities residents need, and Linguistic–cultural relevance (REAL). Com posite scores use severity–exposure dynamic weights in which pillar weights emerge from local demographic exposure and measured severity, addressing an open question raised in the deprivation-index literature; a fixed-weight national reference score is retained for com parability, and the resulting convex severity property is derived explicitly and grounded in the Foster–Greer–Thorbecke squared-poverty-gap tradition. A 10,000-draw Monte Carlo analysis with tier-scaled independent parameter error and correlated community-level bias yields realistic score precision of approximately ±5–6 points and a formal reporting rule. In an eleven-ZCTA pilot across ten states, two adjacent Cambridge, Massachusetts ZCTAs 0.9 miles apart score 83.2 and 66.0—a 17-point functional-access gap, positive in 99.8% of sim ulation draws, not reproduced by the comparison indices evaluated here. Pilot parameters are disclosed as structured expert estimates calibrated to federal data frameworks; a staged validation agenda (Delphi consensus, criterion regression, resident ground truth) is specified.

title: La-Meme: Distorted Attachment to Anger-Based Authority and the Reproduction of Psychosocial Harm in Workplace Bullying
abstract: This paper contributes to research on workplace bullying, abusive supervision, and psychosocial harm by proposing the concept of La-Meme, or Love-Anger Meme. Existing studies have clarified how hostile supervisory behavior, workplace bullying, workplace incivility, emotional contagion, social learning, organizational culture, psychological safety, and organizational silence harm individuals and organizations. However, less attention has been paid to how anger-based workplace cultures may be preserved not only through fear, imitation, and social learning, but also through distorted attachment to anger-based authority. La-Meme is defined as a distorted attachment pattern in which anger and attachment become mutually entangled within organizational relationships. The concept operates in two directions. First, leaders or authority figures who rely on anger may favor members who imitate their anger, interpreting them as similar others, loyal subordinates, or successors. Second, members harmed by anger may still seek recognition, belonging, renewed proximity, or restored self-worth from the same anger-based authority. Through this ambivalent interdependence, anger-based authority can be preserved not only by intimidation but also by attachment. The paper argues that La-Meme helps explain several forms of psychosocial harm in workplaces, including reduced psychological safety, organizational silence, downward aggression, turnover, distorted evaluation criteria, and the obscuring of responsibility. Rather than treating workplace anger only as individual emotional dysregulation or isolated misconduct, the concept highlights the relational and cultural processes through which harmful anger may be protected, imitated, and affectively maintained. The paper concludes that interventions against workplace bullying should examine not only visible expressions of anger, but also who imitates anger, who protects anger-based imitators, who seeks recognition from anger-based authority, and how distorted attachment sustains harmful workplace patterns.

title: Distributed Mediated Personhood: Relational Continuity and Parasocial Relationship Anchoring in Hybrid Human–Avatar Systems
abstract: Parasocial relationship (PSR) theory was developed for media environments in which embodiment, identity, and agency were typically bundled within a single social figure. VTubers challenge this assumption by separating symbolic embodiment (the avatar) from the human source of agency (the operator), creating a unique opportunity to examine where audiences locate relational continuity when these elements can vary independently. Drawing on survey data from 978 VTuber viewers, this study distinguishes between relationship formation and relationship anchoring as two analytically distinct dimensions of parasocial engagement. The first dimension concerns the relational conditions under which parasocial engagement develops. Correlational analyses indicate that interaction-oriented engagement, emotional gratifications, and community participation are associated with stronger identification, emotional connection, and trust. These findings suggest that parasocial engagement in VTuber environments emerges through many of the same social and affective processes documented in prior PSI/PSR research. The second dimension concerns relationship anchoring: where audiences locate relational continuity once parasocial relationships become established. Across multiple indicators, viewers increasingly oriented toward the human operator over time, were substantially less tolerant of operator replacement than avatar replacement, and expressed strong preferences for human-operated rather than AI-operated virtual performers. Taken together, these patterns suggest that audiences place greater weight on continuity of the operator than on continuity of visual representation alone. Building on these findings, the study proposes a distinction between relationship formation and relationship anchoring and develops the concept of distributed mediated personhood to account for parasocial targets whose embodiment and agency are distributed across multiple components. More broadly, the findings suggest that durable digital intimacy depends less on representation itself than on the perception of relational continuity across interactions. As mediated personhood becomes increasingly distributed across human and artificial systems, the findings suggest that understanding where audiences locate relational continuity constitutes an important extension of PSI/PSR theory in increasingly hybrid human–avatar–AI environments.

title: Mamism: Anger-Based Communication, Organizational Ideology, and the Internalization of Workplace Anger Culture
abstract: This paper proposes the concept of Mamism as a theoretical framework for explaining how anger-based workplace communication becomes ideologically internalized as organizational common sense. Mamism is derived from Ma-Meme, meaning Multiple Anger Meme, and ism, indicating an ideological or value-based system. While Ma-Meme explains how anger-centered emotional memes are reproduced through psychological reward, rhetorical justification, and social imitation, Mamism explains how such anger memes become moralized, institutionalized, and internalized through workplace communication. Previous research on abusive supervision, workplace bullying, organizational silence, psychological safety, emotional contagion, organizational culture, institutional theory, ideology, and workplace communication has clarified how anger and hostile conduct harm individuals and organizations. However, these perspectives do not sufficiently explain why anger is sometimes interpreted not as emotional dysregulation or harassment, but as responsibility, seriousness, discipline, leadership, or loyalty to the organization. This paper addresses that gap by examining how organizational language, interactional patterns, evaluative norms, and communicative expectations transform anger from an emotional behavior into a moralized workplace ideology. The central argument is that Mamism emerges when anger is no longer merely used or imitated, but believed in. Under Mamism, anger becomes a communicative ideology. Anger is reinterpreted as instruction, intimidation as leadership, public correction as education, silence as discipline, and fear as productive tension. As a result, resistance to anger may be interpreted as weakness, immaturity, irresponsibility, or disruption of order. Mamism thereby produces organizational silence, reduces psychological safety, distorts emotional currency, legitimizes harassment, selects for anger-adaptive actors, and devalues non-anger-based communication. The paper argues that escaping Mamism requires more than emotional regulation training or anti-harassment education. Organizations must reduce the symbolic and practical value of anger within workplace communication, refuse to treat anger as evidence of competence or responsibility, and create conditions in which dialogue, explanation, repair, humor, psychological safety, and non-anger-based control are organizationally valued. The problem is not anger itself, but the communicative and ideological structure through which anger becomes believed in as organizational morality.

title: Fantasy Engagement & Sleep quality among Binge-watchers
abstract: Watching multiple episodes for a long period using different streaming platforms such as Netflix, Viki, Wetv, Hulu, has become widespread in recent years. The interest in Korean pop music, dramas and movies have also increased widely among youth during the recent years. There is much attention paid to Binge-watching behaviour, but its relations to Fantasy Engagement and Sleep quality among K-content watchers during Covid-19 has dearth of research. So, this present study sought to fill this gap by investigating the sleep quality and fantasy engagement of college students who binge-watch K-contents during the covid-19 pandemic. Purposive sampling method was used in the study, and a sample of 100 female adult respondents were collected. Binge-Watching Engagement and Symptoms Questionnaire (BWESQ), Sleep Quality scale (SQS) and Fantasy Engagement scale were used to assess the variables. To find the relationship and difference between the variables, Pearson’s product moment correlation and independent t-test were used. The findings of the study indicated that high Binge-watching behaviour leads to Fantasy Engagement and acute sleep problems.

title: The evolution of punk: From Rebellion to Retail
authors: Anika Dhingra, Gokul Prabhu, Ananta Ganjoo
abstract: As a working-class subculture with strong political roots, the punk ideology emerged in the 1970s in opposition to establishment politics and consumer culture. Thus, at first, punk was both a cultural revolution and a tool for political self-expression. This article analyzes how the politicized punk subculture turned into a commercial aesthetic phenomenon that operates in today’s capitalist society. Using the period approach, this review article analyzes punk’s development from an underground phenomenon of the 1970s, to its politicization in the 1980s, massification in the 1990s, diversification in the 2000s, and reinvention in the digital era of the 2010s and 2020s. The primary focus is on showing how media images, globalization, and the fashion industry transformed the visual elements of punk culture into mere icons divorced from their ideological background. Theories such as “Subcultural Zoo” explain the mechanisms employed by capitalistic economies in the process of appropriation of subcultures, thus turning their distinctive traits into standardized commodities. Additionally, the study points out the socio-economic and moral consequences of such a transformation, notably, the marginalization of working-class subjects and the use of oppressive modes of production. Regardless of its commodification, this paper asserts that the essence of punk ideology remains alive in alternative settings, especially through grassroot efforts and online forums. In the end, the study defines punk ideology as a living and ever-changing concept with different interpretations.

title: Who Overreports? Regime Support and Preference Falsification in Iran
authors: Daniel L. Tavana, Kevan Harris, chi shun fong, Amir Farmanesh
abstract: In authoritarian contexts, individuals often express greater support for the regime in public than they hold in private. This gap---overreporting---is typically treated as a measurement problem rather than a political behavior to be explained. We argue that overreporting deserves greater attention in survey-based studies of regime support and is itself shaped by which social referents are salient to respondents during survey interviews. We develop and test an original theoretical framework that identifies three sources of overreporting: expressive identification, behavioral conformity, and civic resources. Drawing on a pre-registered, nationally representative telephone survey of residents of Iran (N=1,999) with an embedded double list experiment, we estimate regime support from the list experiment at 47 percent---roughly ten percentage points below the 57 percent recorded by direct questioning. Our findings indicate that expressive identification matters most: national pride and religiosity both predict substantial overreporting. Preference falsification in Iran is shaped less by the coercion citizens face from the state than by the identity-based attachments the regime encourages---findings with implications for how scholars measure and interpret regime support in closed societies.

title: Personality Emergence and Adaptive Functioning: An Enactive, Complex-Dynamical Systems Account
abstract: Enactive psychiatry conceptualizes mental disorders as maladaptive modes of functioning that emerge within the recursive coupling between an embodied agent and their world. This chapter advances an integrative framework synthesizing enactive principles with complex dynamical systems theory to illustrate how personality operates as the foundational architecture of these functional modes. Personality is conceptualized as an evolving topography of attractor landscapes or stylistic regularities, structuring multi-level sense-making across nested timescales. Fundamental to this account is adaptive flexibility, the metastable capacity of a system to balance structural coherence and environmental exploration. The multi-level topology of individual differences is mapped through the traits and facets of the Five-Factor Model, with developmental pathways traced to early temperament and interaction patterns, reframed here as sedimented relational topologies of structural coupling. Personality difficulties or maladaptive functioning is understood as global alterations in control topology, characterized either by pathological rigidity and high inertia, or systemic fragmentation and high instability. Finally, this framework operationalizes enactive theory through an empirical turn, utilizing real-time tracking methodologies and time series analyses to capture intrapersonal and interpersonal coordination. Clinical change is comprehended as context sensitive perturbations that facilitate systemic reorganization, providing a conceptually coherent and clinically actionable science of the person.

title: Risky Business: On the politicisation of risk in European AI standardization
abstract: Technical standards are supposed to become a key material infrastructure and regulatory mecha-nism in global AI governance. Nonetheless, there are only few empirically grounded studies, researching the inherent interest politics and value conflicts within the design and development process of AI standards. Drawing on fieldwork conducted between 2023 and 2026, including interviews with standardisation experts, regulators, and standards-developing organisations, analysis of standards documents and reports, and participant observation in committees and online communities, this paper examines how AI is translated into technical standards and con-structed as a regulatory object. In contrast to approaches that emphasise the depoliticising effects of standardisation practices, the findings reveal an emerging politicisation of the field. Rather than merely implementing regulatory objectives, standardisers engage in divergent conflicts over competing visions of AI. Empirically, the paper identifies three classification conflicts concerning categorisation, measurement, and interference. While risk serves as a central organising principle for classifying AI systems, its practical enactment remains contested and the standardisation process itself remains risky as its outcomes are uncertain and contested. By outlining these conflicts, this paper inter-venes in the growing yet heterogeneous debate on AI governance in practice. The findings suggest that ongoing conflicts within AI standardisation may significantly influence the future implementation, legitimacy, and effectiveness of EU AI governance.

title: What the North Can Learn from the South Sex Work, Self-Organisation, and Extraterritorial Power in the Global South. A Theoretical Synthesis.
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work in the Global South and argues for an inverted learning gradient. Where an earlier companion paper showed that in the Global North the future of sex work is decided in labour law and in digital and financial infrastructure that the state would have to provide, this paper shows that in the Global South the protective infrastructure exists where sex workers have built it themselves - as peer organisation, cooperative banking, and community health networks, often before and without the state. Drawing on peer-reviewed research and movement scholarship - including Mgbako's fieldwork on African sex worker activism, the literature on the Sonagachi project and the Durbar collective in India, research on the Latin American network RedTraSex, and Agustin's analysis of the 'rescue industry' - the paper makes three claims. First, the degree of self-organisation, not the legal framework, is the strongest predictor of protective capacity in the South. Second, the same extraterritorial US power that regulates the North through platform law operates in the South through funding and visa policy, exemplified by the PEPFAR anti-prostitution pledge, the 2012 visa denials that forced an alternative AIDS conference to Kolkata, and the 2025 dismantling of PEPFAR itself - which strikes hardest at precisely those structures that had made themselves dependent on it. Third, the learning gradient between North and South runs in both directions: the South can learn institutional permanence from the North, the North can learn self-built infrastructure and peer efficiency from the South. South Africa's ongoing decriminalisation process serves as the Southern counterpart to the Belgian experiment. Related work https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/uc57b_v1

title: Data-driven Chinese investment and trade in Europe beyond Huawei and TikTok
abstract: Data access and related security implications have become a frequent concern in Chinese companies’ global expansion. This paper argues that the intensifying debate over the national security implications of Chinese companies’ data access abroad lacks a more nuanced understanding of the types of data accessed. Using a typology of four data types: transaction, product, process, and transmission and storage data, access to data by Chinese companies in Germany is empirically investigated. The analysis shows that venture capital investments, mergers and acquisitions, greenfield investments, and trade provide access to different types of data. Trade is the most important internationalization strategy for access to data, and product data is the most frequently accessed data type. This has several implications for the debate over the security risks posed by Chinese companies’ access to data in Europe. Trade requires little physical presence in Germany; data access can be achieved without ownership of large-scale physical infrastructure. It reveals a mismatch between the predominant regulatory focus on mergers and acquisitions and trade as the primary strategy for data access, which is less regulated. Trade, as the central data access strategy, also implies that much depends on the market success of China’s products and services.

title: Exploring the mobilities of Kenyan older people living in informal settlements using a capability approach
authors: Carlo Luiu, Amos Wandera, Vera Bukachi, Rosie Day
abstract: Despite significant discussions on transport inequalities in African cities, older people's mobility needs remain under-researched. To gain a deeper understanding of such issues, this study focuses on older people living in Kenyan informal settlements and uses the capability approach to investigate their options for mobility, activities and locations, barriers, coping strategies and unfulfilled mobility needs. The study employs a qualitative approach comprising four focus groups with 24 participants and a follow-on community engagement workshop with people aged above 50 years old living in the informal settlement of Kibera in Nairobi. The results indicate that older people face significant challenges in their everyday mobility. Living with poor health conditions, level of poverty, lack and poor quality of walking infrastructure, personal safety and ageist behaviour are the main factors shaping and affecting their mobility. The capability approach offers a framework to understand older people’s mobility practices and implications for wellbeing. It enables an analysis of how mobility is shaped by their needs, constraints and decision-making processes, and how this translates into participation in life. It also offers an exploration of how mobility enables and supports a wider capability set, outlining its multidimensional social impacts.

title: The Scarcity Theory of Specialization: Polymathy, the Architecture of Knowledge, and the Question Posed by Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Modern science policy, professional training, and the research university share a foundational assumption: excellence requires specialization. This article argues that the assumption rests on a category error. Specialization is a rational adaptation to the cognitive scarcity of individual human minds, not an intrinsic property of high-quality cognition, and a civilization that mistakes the adaptation for the ideal will misread both its intellectual history and its technological present. Drawing on the economics of innovation, studies of creative eminence, and the sociology of science, the article develops a scarcity theory of specialization and tests it against three bodies of evidence: the recombinant structure of major discoveries, the documented liabilities of deep expertise, and the avocational breadth of eminent scientists. The article then disaggregates specialization into three distinct functions, cognitive economy, social coordination, and epistemic certification, and argues that large-scale artificial intelligence dissolves only the first. Foundation models constitute a natural experiment demonstrating that breadth and depth can be jointly scaled when memory and time constraints are relaxed. Four falsifiable propositions and implications for doctoral education and research funding follow. The deeper principle organizing knowledge, the article concludes, is not division but integration.

title: When the Standard Becomes the Shield: SA 600, Group Audit Failures, and the Case for Convergence with ISA 600 in India
abstract: This paper examines whether, and how, the design of India's group auditing standard, SA 600 (2002), shaped the interpretive conditions in which audit deficiencies arose in corporate failures, and evaluates the case for convergence with ISA 600. The paper draws on documentary analysis of fifty NFRA enforcement outputs — audit quality review reports (AQRRs) and disciplinary orders issued between 2019 and 2024 across six public interest entities — supplemented by NFRA meeting minutes, the regulator's 2024 consultation on a revised standard, and thirty-two comment letters. The analysis is situated against the international literature on group audit quality and divided auditor responsibility. NFRA's enforcement record reveals a consistent pattern: principal auditors treated SA 600 (2002)'s division of responsibility clause as a basis for limiting their engagement responsibility for subsidiaries and associates, even where fraud indicators were visible. The dominant failures were of implementation — non-compliance with requirements SA 600 already imposed — rather than of design; yet the standard's design structured the interpretive environment so that non-compliance became predictable and defensible. The Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI) did not submit a response to NFRA's public consultation, which effectively constitutes an institutional impasse; while most respondents supported revision, practitioners were divided. Although NFRA's enforcement outputs have featured in Indian professional commentary, they have not previously been mobilised as a systematic empirical corpus in the group audit literature, and this paper is among the first to do so. It demonstrates that standard divergence can create interpretive conditions in which non-compliance becomes institutionally defensible. This has implications for jurisdictions weighing whether and how far to converge national group auditing standards with international requirements on auditor responsibility.

title: Are the children of homeowners increasingly advantaged? Trends in relative and absolute mobility of homeownership in Europe
authors: Selçuk Bedük, Enrico Benassi, Philipp M. Lersch
abstract: Homeownership is declining among younger generations across Europe. A common assumption is that this decline has been increasingly stratified by parental homeownership, due to rising house prices and the growing importance of parental financial support. We show that this assumption does not hold for the average European (and for most countries). Using data from EU-SILC 2011 and 2019 covering 24 European countries, we examine trends in relative and absolute mobility of homeownership for birth cohorts 1951 to 1984. On average, relative mobility remained largely stable across cohorts, while absolute mobility increased. The apparent stability of relative mobility masks a deeper structural cohort shift: downward mobility increased from 14 per cent to 38 per cent, while upward mobility declined from 23 per cent to 8 per cent between cohorts born in the early 1950s and early 1980s. Therefore, homeownership has declined significantly for both children of homeowners and renters. We also compare absolute and relative mobility trends across countries and show that they often diverge for two reasons. First, a mechanical effect of the origin distribution: in countries with widespread parental homeownership, relative mobility largely reflects changes in upward mobility. Second, the source of mobility: using two novel counterfactual simulations, we show that observed absolute mobility changes mainly stem from structural shifts in homeownership chances, rather than strengthening parent-child associations.

title: Mathematics of Forever Wars
abstract: In this paper, we use a popular game-theoretical model, called the Game of Chicken, to generate a random walk in the plane that seems to model well the forever wars of the present, and of the past. The main point of the paper is to show that simple game-theoretical models have a lot of predictive power. The second point is that quantum game algorithms may offer solutions to social problems humanity was not yet able to see.

title: Gender, Populist Attitudes and Voting Behaviour: A Critical Revisitation of Spierings & Zaslove (2017) in Light of EES 2014–2024 Evidence
abstract: This article provides a systematic critical reassessment and empirical update of Spierings and Zaslove's (2017) seminal study on the gender gap in populist voting, integrating a decade of subsequent scholarship and comparative evidence from the European Election Studies (EES) of 2014 and 2024. The original work posited that female socialization toward consensus and conflict aversion structurally distanced women from the antagonistic rhetoric of populist parties, particularly those of the populist radical right (PRR). Drawing on nine-country EES 2014 data and a fifteen-country EES 2024 dataset, we demonstrate that the aggregate PRR gender gap has narrowed considerably — from an average of −5.7 percentage points in 2014 to −3.7 points in 2024 — while exhibiting pronounced cross-national heterogeneity. We attribute this convergence to three partially overlapping mechanisms: (1) the strategic de-demonization of PRR parties under female leadership, producing a 'maternal-authoritarian' aesthetic that lowers women's psychological costs of voting radical right; (2) the emergence of femonationalism as a gendered discursive register that reframes nativist and anti-immigration stances as pro-women positions; and (3) a generational re-sorting, whereby the gender cleavage has migrated from the general electorate to Generation Z, where male respondents support PRR parties at rates 15 percentage points above their female counterparts, while young women increasingly anchor the populist radical left (PRL). These findings challenge the passive socialization model and call for analytical frameworks that treat female agency as constitutive of contemporary populism rather than merely reactive to it.

title: Orphaned Knowledge: A Custodial Theory of Authorship for the Age of Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Scholarly publishing and intellectual property law have converged on a similar rule for generative AI: a machine cannot be an author because it cannot be accountable, and outputs without a human creator do not fit established regimes of protection or credit. This article argues that these responses are sound but conceptually incomplete because they treat authorship mainly as a matter of origination and reward. Drawing on Foucault, Merton, Shapin, and Hardwig, I argue that authorship has long bundled three functions: origination, reward, and custodianship. Generative AI disrupts that bundle by separating origination from the forward-looking human work of defending, developing, teaching, remembering, and answering for knowledge over time. The result is what I call orphaned knowledge: findings that may be true and valuable yet lack any identifiable person committed to keeping them alive. The article makes two contributions. First, it shows that both the credit economy of journals and the property economy of copyright are structured around human knowers, leaving machine-originated knowledge in a double vacuum. Second, it advances a custodial theory of authorship that redefines the author’s signature less as a retrospective claim to credit than as a prospective commitment to steward a finding. On this view, a machine may originate, but only a human or institution can undertake custody. The age of machine discovery therefore forces scholarship to confront a prior question that earlier authorship debates have obscured: not only who made knowledge, but who will keep it.

title: An inductive-deductive circular methodology of the research process: Systematic thinking from prior assumptions to final synthesis
abstract: In this essay, we reflect on the circularity of social science research and how we can attempt to put this circularity into a system to help structure our thinking and research practices through structured inductive-deductive reflection. We present our model for thinking of research as a holistic process from making our prior assumptions concrete and updating them systematically through constant comparison and a circular inductive-deductive analysis, towards a synthesis of the research findings.

title: Contained and Uncontained Experiments
abstract: I introduce a typology of political science experiments based on their ``containedness." Contained experiments are those for which it is plausible to ``perform the same procedure" and expect ``the same result." Uncontained experiments are those for which practitioners expect intrinsic variation to dominate; even when performing the same procedure, the goal is to understand how results differ across time and context. I propose three conceptions of containedness: pragmatic (community expectations about replication), complexity (information required to specify the procedure), and temporal (speed of experimentation relative to drift in the phenomenon). Lab experiments are generally the most contained, survey experiments occupy a middle position, and field experiments are the least contained. I provide a diagnostic rubric for evaluating containedness and argue that distinct evidentiary strategies are appropriate at different levels. For highly uncontained experiments, ``replication" is not a coherent goal; knowledge accumulation must proceed through theory rather than induction.

title: Genetic Mirroring in Adoptees: Toward a Formal Construct Definition, Nomological Network, and Operationalization Framework
abstract: Genetic mirroring, the developmentally recurring need to perceive reflected biological similarity in genetically related others, has gained clinical salience as an explanatory framework for the identity experiences of adoptees and donor-conceived individuals. Despite this, the construct lacks the theoretical architecture required for empirical investigation: a precise definition, differentiation from adjacent constructs, and a plausible operationalization pathway. The present paper addresses that gap through four coordinated contributions. First, a formal construct definition is proposed, encompassing three theoretically distinct dimensions: biological salience, embodied discontinuity, and mirroring responsiveness. Second, the construct is situated within a nomological network specifying its hypothesized relationships with established psychological variables, including identity exploration, narrative identity coherence, attachment security, genealogical bewilderment, and physical self-concept. Third, a theoretical case is made for incremental validity over existing frameworks: genetic mirroring is proposed to capture variance in adoptee psychological functioning that neither attachment theory nor narrative identity frameworks can fully account for. Fourth, a preliminary scale structure is proposed as proof of concept. The paper's aim is to transform a clinically emergent construct into a formally theorizable and measurable entity, enabling the empirical program necessary to evaluate its scientific and practical significance.

title: Hedging Under U.S. Reindustrialization: Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia in an Era of Intensifying U.S.–China Rivalry
abstract: Southeast Asian states maintain deep economic ties with both the United States and China, and the region has become a major destination for supply-chain relocation from firms in both powers. Recent U.S. reindustrialization policies—including the extension of elevated tariffs to Southeast Asian exports—pose new challenges to the region's export-led growth model and to U.S. strategic positioning in the Indo-Pacific. Drawing on hedging theory, particularly Cheng-Chwee Kuik's distinction between return-maximizing and risk-contingency behaviors, this paper examines how three manufacturing-oriented, trade-dependent economies—Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia—adjusted their foreign-policy and geoeconomic orientations in response to successive waves of U.S. reindustrialization from 2017 to 2026. Using a theory-driven event-coding approach, the study analyzes 66 verified foreign-policy and geoeconomic events to trace directional tilts toward Washington, Beijing, or neutrality across three U.S. administrations. The findings show that U.S. reindustrialization has not uniformly pushed Southeast Asian states toward China. Instead, different phases of U.S. policy produced qualitatively distinct hedging responses, mediated by structural factors that vary across countries. The analysis also reveals a misalignment between U.S. tariff instruments and the goals of the Indo-Pacific Strategy: tariff shocks create openings for Chinese economic leverage in the very economies U.S. industrial policy seeks to draw closer. The durability of the Indo-Pacific Strategy will depend on whether Washington can align its economic tools with its security ambitions and provide the structural conditions that enable partners to hedge coherently under intensifying great-power competition.

title: eHealth Use among Adults with Chronic Illness in the United States: Health Self-Efficacy and Health Literacy as Pathways to Mental Well-Being
authors: Qianfeng Lu, Wen Jiao, Qiaofei Jovi Wu
abstract: Patients with chronic illness face a considerable mental health burden. However, the pathways through which eHealth use influences mental health in this population remain poorly understood. Drawing on Street's (2003) three-stage model, this study examined the effects of eHealth use on mental health among chronic illness patients, with health self-efficacy as a mediator and health literacy as a moderator. Data from the Health Information National Trends Survey 7 (HINTS 7) were analysed among U.S. adults with chronic illness (N = 2,928) using the PROCESS macro. The direct effect of eHealth use on mental health was insignificant. eHealth use was negatively associated with health self-efficacy, which was then positively linked to mental health. Health literacy moderated the relationship between eHealth use and health self-efficacy, with the negative association significant at lower literacy levels but diminishing and trending positive at higher literacy levels. Consequently, the indirect effect of eHealth use on mental health through health self-efficacy was contingent on health literacy, being negative at lower levels and diminishing as literacy increased. These findings suggest that health literacy determines whether eHealth use burdens patients with chronic illness. Therefore, ensuring adequate patients health literacy is important in eHealth interventions targeting this population.

title: Human Brokers in Machine Times: The Network Architecture of Journalism from COVID-19 to the AI Era
authors: James Jaehoon Lee, Jeffrey Layne Blevins, Jeremy D. Gilbert, Joe Germuska
abstract: The dominant narrative of journalism in the digital age has focused on institutional decline and audience fragmentation. However, during the COVID-19 pandemic, we find that news organizations surprisingly reemerged as central actors within digital information ecosystems, serving as network brokers between fragmented online communities. Five years later, we conducted a 6,000-respondent cross-national survey to show that news-mediated brokerage remains durable in the era of AI models, but individual content creators share the brokerage role through which distinct publics come into contact. Here we perform a longitudinal analysis of the changing role of news organizations from 2020 to 2025, by combining network analysis of 141 million tweets from 24 million users during 2020, 45 qualitative interviews with younger news consumers across the United States, Nigeria, and India, and a 6000 person YouGov survey fielded in June 2025 across the US, UK, India, Nigeria, Brazil, and South Korea. Using network analysis and the concept of brokerage, we show that journalism operates as a connective layer in network architecture, channeling information flow across ideologically and geographically distinct communities. We identify a platform-native model of journalism that departs from traditional article formats in favor of contextualized, multimodal, and shareable content. Our findings suggest that in the era of digital platforms, the conventional journalistic product of the article gives way to a distributed model of sense-making, in which journalism's value lies in brokering connections rather than delivering monolithic event narratives. By 2025, the brokerage function persists but the brokers themselves have shifted. Individual creators now share the bridging labor that news organizations performed alone in 2020. These findings suggest a new account of journalism as a dynamic connective broker within networked public life and contemporary information ecosystems.

title: bqmm: Bayesian Multilevel Quantile Regression in R
abstract: Quantile regression describes how covariates shift the conditional quantiles of an outcome, not merely its mean, and is indispensable when effects are heterogeneous across the response distribution. When data are clustered or longitudinal, a mixed-effects formulation is needed. I present bqmm, an R package for Bayesian multilevel quantile regression built on the asymmetric Laplace working likelihood and Stan. The package offers an lme4-style formula interface with nested and crossed random effects, optional LKJ-correlated random effects, estimation of one or several quantiles in a single call, post-hoc non-crossing rearrangement, and a transparent menu of fixed-effect interval methods — the naive posterior, the Yang–Wang–He (2016) sandwich correction, and the infinitesimal jackknife — because the asymmetric Laplace likelihood is misspecified and naive credible intervals can be invalid. The paper describes the model and software design, illustrates usage on longitudinal growth data, summarises a validation study (parameter recovery, simulation-based calibration, and a coverage study), and compares bqmm with related software.

title: Beyond Individual Vulnerability: A European, Critical Work and Organizational Psychology Perspective on Work Addiction in the Digital Workplace.
abstract: Adopting a European vantage point, both empirically and with respect to the regulatory context, this position paper argues that work addiction in digitally mediated work cannot be adequately understood as a matter of individual vulnerability or as a mechanical effect of technology. Drawing on behavioural-addiction models and on critical work and organizational psychology, it advances a psychosocial and practice-oriented reading in which compulsive work emerges from the interaction of personal dispositions, organizational cultures, role demands, digital tools, and recognition systems. The paper distinguishes work addiction from work engagement and from heavy work investment, locating the construct in the combination of excess and compulsion rather than in working hours. It then reframes digital tools (email, messaging, collaborative platforms, and the smartphone) not as neutral instruments but as facilitators, amplifiers, and maintenance environments, connecting the argument to constructs the field has already named, including workplace telepressure, low psychological detachment, technostress, and the autonomy paradox. The argument is extended to the still-fragmented, partly worker-led adoption of artificial intelligence at work, drawing on European and OECD evidence that points to task intensification rather than wholesale displacement, and to governance that lags adoption. A central claim is that organizations frequently reward continuous availability as professionalism, normalizing compulsion as a virtue and obscuring it as a psychosocial risk. Against this backdrop, recent European and Italian developments on the right to disconnect are read as encouraging but partial signals. The paper closes with propositions for a critical-practice agenda, framing the right to disconnect as an organizational competence rather than an individual hygiene practice.

title: Public perceptions of climate policy fairness and effectiveness across climate policy types and domains in 15 countries
authors: Nina L. Frings, Ulf J.J. Hahnel
abstract: Individuals are more supportive of climate policies when they think the measures are fair and effective. This evidence, however, is primarily based on data from Western samples, leaving it open how public effectiveness and fairness perceptions vary across geographical contexts and how they are shaped by regional indicators. Here, we aim to address these questions, investigating climate policy effectiveness and fairness perceptions across four policy types and two policy domains in 15 countries, covering all continents (N=6’052). Effectiveness and fairness ratings varied between policy types and domains, and this pattern was relatively stable across nations. However, individuals in the Global South were overall more optimistic about the effectiveness of climate policies and evaluated them as generally fairer compared to the Global North. Linking psychological and contextual data further shows that effectiveness and fairness perceptions were higher in countries that are more vulnerable to climate change impacts and lower in countries with higher per capita emissions. Moreover, the impact of psychological variables such as climate change concern and political orientation on both outcomes varied as a function of national context. Taken together, our findings point to important geographical asymmetries in the extent to which the public perceives climate mitigation policies as effective and fair, shaped by how much countries contribute to and are affected by climate change.

title: What voters think and feel about a climate law: cognitive–affective mental models during a Swiss national referendum
authors: Nina L. Frings, Julius Fenn, Samuel Aeschbach, Rui Mata, Ulf J.J. Hahnel
abstract: Lacking public support remains a major barrier to ambitious climate policy, yet little is known about how citizens mentally represent specific policy proposals and whether or how these representations change over time. We assessed voters’ cognitive– affective mental models of a real-world climate policy proposal, the 2023 Swiss Climate Protection Law, at two time points preceding a national referendum on the policy. A sample of Swiss voters (N=254) drew their mental models by adding their own cognitive associations and affective evaluations towards the law one month before and immediately prior to the referendum. Opposers were more likely to make reference to financial or personal costs and to rate concepts more negatively, whereas supporters were more likely to make reference to climate and future protection concepts and to rate concepts more positively. While the cognitive associations differed across voter groups, the complexity of mental models, indicated by the number of provided concepts and their interconnectedness, did not. Both opposers and supporters reported more cost-related concepts at the second time point but there was no strong evidence of increased complexity or changes in affective evaluations over time. Crucially, affective evaluations were the strongest predictor of final policy support relative to cognitive associations. These results suggest that while political campaigns may shape the considerations citizens associate with a policy, policy support does not necessarily reflect differences in the complexity of these representations and may be more closely associated with their affective evaluation than with their cognitive content.

title: Upfront Pricing or In-Game Purchases? Monetizing Content in Video Games
abstract: A video game developer can monetize its content (maps, themes, modes) in two ways: upfront pricing, a single high up-front price for the game with all content included, or in-game purchases, a free or cheap base with each content piece sold separately. We model this as a choice between bundling and separately selling a content stream and ask when in-game purchases dominate. Upfront pricing wins when content tastes are dispersed and weakly correlated; in-game purchases win when tastes are strongly correlated or pieces diﬀer in appeal, when risk-averse players face uncertain content, and when players are present-biased or impulsive. A simulation with all forces active shows that bundling is the benchmark optimum and that each friction shifts the boundary toward in-game purchases, with behavioral forces moving it most. The framework explains why premium console and PC games are still sold up front, while long-lived games, especially mobile games, rely on in-game purchases.

title: Benchmarking the Personalization Capabilities of Large Language Models
authors: Vinay Aggarwal, Yaman K Singla, Ashutosh Srivastava, Siddharth Yedlapati, Shashwat Dixit, Jitendra Ajmera, Balaji Krishnamurthy
abstract: Personalization, the act of varying a message to induce action from a specific receiver while keeping sender, channel, and time fixed, has a long tradition in psychology and marketing as a two-party problem in which sender and receiver have independent objectives. Large language models remove the bounded-inventory constraint of classical retrieval-and-ranking approaches by generating a continuum of message variants conditioned on inferred receiver state, raising the question of how well current models perform personalization in the classical sense. Existing LLM personalization benchmarks measure sender-side adaptation, in which the receiver is the same user the model is serving. The two-party question, whether a generated message induces its intended action in a third party, has been investigated only through A/B tests and small-scale human studies that cannot be re-run against a new model on demand. We adapt the Bayesian Persuasion framework of Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011) to generative agents and instantiate the formulation in sales, where receiver actions are routinely logged against the outreach that induced them. We release SDR-Bench, a public corpus of 6,279 customer success stories spanning 22 industries and approximately 200 enterprises, served through a temporally constrained simulation that prevents future-data leakage. Across frontier LLMs and deep-research agents, we observe a consistent personalization plateau and on a Fortune 100 tech cohort no model statistically separates successful from unsuccessful outreach. A field deployment with 12 professional sales representatives validates the framework, with 48 percent of model-generated content rated immediately useful and senior-expert agreement at Pearson 0.82. We release SDR-Arena and SDR-Bench publicly to support reproducible study of generative personalization at scale.

title: When, and how much, might effect estimates be biased when analysing mixed-mode survey data? The roles of mode effects, mode selection, and mode split.
authors: Georgia D Tomova, Richard J. Silverwood, Liam Wright
abstract: Survey data are increasingly collected using mixed-mode designs (e.g. personal interview and web questionnaire). Little is known, however, about the extent to which this introduces bias in subsequent analyses, or whether simply including mode as a covariate addresses it. Using data simulations, we identified the conditions under which mode effects (mode measurement differences) and mode selection (respondent differences) introduce bias, complementing this with empirical illustrations using mixed-mode data from the 1958 National Child Development Study. In simulations, absent mode selection, substantial bias arose only from unusually large mode effects, but was amplified when mode selection was present. Controlling for mode under strong mode selection introduced substantial bias, including sign-reversal of the estimate. Bias was more pronounced when the sample was split equally between modes. The direction and size of bias will depend on the direction of all effects. In our empirical illustration, the results were largely unchanged after controlling for mode, possibly reflecting weaker mode selection and mode effects in these data. These findings highlight that, while possible, substantial bias from mixed-mode designs may be unlikely in many practical settings. However, the risk of bias, and the appropriate strategy to address it, should be considered on an analysis-specific basis.

title: Buying time, doing family? Domestic outsourcing and time use in Sweden
authors: Betiel Selassie, Elien Dalman, Lisa Eklund, Therese Nilsson
abstract: Domestic outsourcing is often presented as a way to reduce unpaid work, ease work-family conflict, and promote gender equality. However, it remains unclear whether outsourcing domestic services changes how individuals divide their time between paid work, housework, childcare, and other forms of unpaid work. This paper examines the relationship between domestic outsourcing and time use in Sweden, a strong dual-earner context in which the RUT tax deduction subsidizes half of domestic services costs. We link the 2021 Swedish Time Use Survey to administrative register data on household-level use of RUT services to reliably capture outsourcing incidence and intensity. This linkage allows us to examine how domestic outsourcing associates with time spent on paid work and types of unpaid work, and how these associations differ by gender and parenthood status. The results indicate that outsourcing associates with differences in time allocation above a relatively high threshold of regular use, but that the relationship between outsourcing and time use depends on family type. Regular domestic outsourcing associates with a pattern consistent with time allocation theory among individuals without resident children. Non-parenting men and women who regularly outsource spend substantially more time on paid work and less time on unpaid work than otherwise similar non-outsourcers. Reductions in unpaid work are concentrated in routine housework, the activity most directly substitutable. Among parents, by contrast, regular outsourcing does not associate with more time in paid work nor clear reductions in total unpaid work. Mothers show some reduction in routine housework, accompanied by increases in non-routine housework, while fathers in regularly outsourcing households do not spend less time on unpaid work, including routine housework. The results suggest that domestic outsourcing cannot be understood simply as buying time for paid work. Among parents it may supplement rather than replace own unpaid work and may thus help sustain intensive combinations of employment, caregiving and other parts of domestic life. The analysis also reveals discrepancies between register-based and self-reported measures of outsourcing. The study contributes to research on gender, outsourcing, and work-family dynamics by showing that the meaning of “buying time” varies by gender and parenthood.

title: Inclusive by design: a three-pillar framework for equitable online conferences
authors: Ebi Antony George, Natacha Rossi, Alexis Buatois, Valentin Lecheval
abstract: Scientific conferences remain central to scholarly communication, yet participation is constrained by several systemic barriers. Although online conferencing expanded rapidly during the COVID-19 pandemic, there remains limited evidence regarding which organisational practices effectively support equitable participation and sustained engagement. Here, we examine Animal Behaviour Live (ABL), a multi-year international online conference series, as a case study in inclusive conference design. We propose a framework structured around three organisational pillars – who participates, who presents, and who organises – combined with an iterative evaluation of participant engagement. For each pillar, we identify systemic barriers to inclusion and discuss targeted organisational strategies derived from our experience across four conference editions. Synthesising demographic data, platform analytics, and simulations of abstract selection outcomes, we show that ABL sustains broad international participation and long-term asynchronous engagement while increasing representation across geographic regions, gender, and career stages. We further demonstrate that a continent-stratified abstract selection process actively mitigates psychological self-selection biases, such as a gendered confidence gap in format preference. This process also outperformed random or score-only selection approaches in simultaneously balancing scientific quality, gender representation, geographic diversity, and inclusion of researchers self-reporting lower access to the international scientific community. Our findings establish that equitable representation in online conferences does not emerge by default and provide an evidence-informed framework with actionable strategies for designing more inclusive international scientific conferences.

title: Was der Norden vom Süden lernen kann Sexarbeit, Selbstorganisation und extraterritoriale Macht im Globalen Süden. Eine theoretische Synthese.
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work in the Global South and argues for an inverted learning gradient. Where an earlier companion paper showed that in the Global North the future of sex work is decided in labour law and in digital and financial infrastructure that the state would have to provide, this paper shows that in the Global South the protective infrastructure exists where sex workers have built it themselves - as peer organisation, cooperative banking, and community health networks, often before and without the state. Drawing on peer-reviewed research and movement scholarship - including Mgbako's fieldwork on African sex worker activism, the literature on the Sonagachi project and the Durbar collective in India, research on the Latin American network RedTraSex, and Agustin's analysis of the 'rescue industry' - the paper makes three claims. First, the degree of self-organisation, not the legal framework, is the strongest predictor of protective capacity in the South. Second, the same extraterritorial US power that regulates the North through platform law operates in the South through funding and visa policy, exemplified by the PEPFAR anti-prostitution pledge, the 2012 visa denials that forced an alternative AIDS conference to Kolkata, and the 2025 dismantling of PEPFAR itself - which strikes hardest at precisely those structures that had made themselves dependent on it. Third, the learning gradient between North and South runs in both directions: the South can learn institutional permanence from the North, the North can learn self-built infrastructure and peer efficiency from the South. South Africa's ongoing decriminalisation process serves as the Southern counterpart to the Belgian experiment. Related work https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/df43j_v1

title: Politics of Association and Evaluative Logics in the Climate Contrarian Blogosphere
abstract: This study provides an investigation of the processes of legitimation and evaluative logics of the climate contrarian blogosphere. Attention is a scarce resource in information rich environments. Drawing on theoretical frameworks from Bourdieu’s Field Theory and Science and Technology Studies, hyperlinks are conceptualised as offering an insight into the enacted epistemological and axiological preferences of climate sceptic bloggers as these digital objects trace who is deemed worthy of attention and what kinds of knowledge practices these targeted sources represent. Data was collected from the blogrolls of 198 climate sceptic and lukewarmer blogs that were sampled by a snowball method to generate a final dataset of 857 nodes/websites with 2628 edges/hyperlinks. These nodes were thematically classified to identify types of websites and sources. Analysis of network composition showed the most prominent sources on the climate contrarian blogosphere were climate sceptic sources (35.1%), right-wing sources (16.7%), and scientific sources (12.3%). Further node-level models and exponential random graph models were employed to estimate how enacted preferences differed by structural position and influenced tie formation processes. This paper contributes to sociological theories of legitimation by demonstrating how evaluative logics in digital fields of struggle can be reconstructed through the relational traces of enacted preference.

title: Data Centres and the Emergence of the Twin Transition Industrial Complex
authors: Giorgos Bougioukos, Charis Papaevangelou, Eugenia Siapera
abstract: The EU has called the ‘twin transition’ a win-win strategy for tackling climate change and strengthening its digital autonomy amid geopolitical and geoeconomic tensions. However, high-level policy discourse can hide the material frictions, extractive dynamics, and emerging dependencies associated with implementing this agenda. The paper examines Western Macedonia, a region historically defined by lignite extraction, now transforming into Greece’s post-lignite development hub. Drawing on critical data centre studies and critical political economy scholarship, we trace the emergence of a twin transition industrial complex (TTIC), that is a system of a parallel and interlinked development of renewables and large-scale, often AI-ready data centres. Methodologically, the study combines 12 semi-structured interviews with locals, on-site visits, as well as an analysis of relevant policy and corporate communication documents. We discern four key findings: A) Western Macedonia is transformed into a hub for renewable energy and data centres, which will help Greece integrate into the global AI value chain. B) However, this shift is causing the displacement of locals, especially young people, and creating a ‘limbo’ situation that will lead to social and ecological exhaustion. C) The green transition is also creating ‘green enclosures’ and transforming the landscape by converting land, including historically publicly funded PPC land, into assets for a more exclusionary and extractive digital transformation. D) Despite official narratives portraying Western Macedonia as a ‘Greek Silicon Valley’, locals are excluded from decision-making and governance processes, leading to disillusionment and a sense of hopelessness for the future. Our main contribution is the outlining of an emerging twin transition industrial complex, wherein the twin transition and digital transformation are planned and materialised by state and (Big Tech) capitalist actors according to the imperatives of the AI industry’s global value chains at the expense of local communities and ecosystems.

title: Naturalistic measure of social norms alignment
authors: Yevhen Kostiuk, Kenneth Enevoldsen, Peter Bjerregaard Vahlstrup, Márton Kardos, Kristoffer Nielbo
abstract: Social norms reflect shared expectations on acceptable behavior. Measuring social norms alignment remains challenging, with existing approaches typically relying on artificial closed-form evaluations such as multiple-choice questionnaires or measuring agreement with predefined statements. In the context of this work, social norms alignment refers to measuring an agreement between solutions with respect to the social problem or dilemma. We propose a framework for measuring social norm alignment in naturalistic, free-form settings through solution matching. The framework enables us to measure alignment between any two dilemma responses e.g., LLMs to a human, LLMs to LLMs, or human to human. We introduce two metrics: stated and explicit agreement accuracy, and construct a dataset of 3k non-trivial social dilemmas in Danish. All dilemmas are assigned reference solutions derived from three panelists, who serve as culturally grounded judges. We evaluate the agreement of several LLMs and human responses in an interaction setup that resembles natural user-model conversations. Our results show that the proposed metrics produce consistent model rankings and reveal variation in agreement across different types of dilemmas, with higher agreement observed for topics such as neighbor conflicts and shared living situations. Overall, our work introduces a dataset and evaluation framework for studying culturally grounded social reasoning in naturalistic open-ended conversations.

title: Personality traits predict (not) seeking professional help for common mental health problems
authors: Helo Liis Soodla, Kirsti Akkermann, Kadri Kõiv, Kelli Lehto, Estonian Biobank research team, Uku Vainik, René Mõttus
abstract: Personality traits may explain why many people with clinical-level mental health concerns do not seek professional help. Among 52,784 participants in the Estonian Biobank, we examined the personality profiles of individuals experiencing significant depression and anxiety disorder symptoms, comparing those who did and did not seek help, as recorded in a nationwide diagnostic register. We also assessed traits’ overall predictive accuracy for help-seeking. Help-seekers tended to exhibit trait profiles more characteristic of psychopathology. For example, even after adjusting for self-reported depression and anxiety disorder symptoms, they had higher neuroticism and openness, and they specifically reported being more stress-prone and feeling unhealthy. Personality traits substantially predicted help-seeking, with nuances (Area Under Curve, AUC = 0.71) outperforming domains (AUC = 0.63), self-reported psychopathology (AUC = 0.65) and demographic factors (AUC = 0.54). Quick, non-invasive personality trait assessments could help detect those less likely to seek help and contribute to early, targeted interventions.

title: Using agent-based simulations to anticipate risks and mitigation measures in platform governance
authors: Max van Drunen, Natali Helberger, Aytalina Kulichkina, Annie Waldherr
abstract: Risk analysis and mitigation in platform governance are constrained by the difficulties of testing changes to platforms’ systems and accessing data. Other fields, ranging from public health to economics, as well as an emerging body of platform research, use agent-based modeling to overcome this obstacle by empowering researchers and regulators to test changes virtually. However, it is still unclear how and under what conditions agent-based modeling can effectively support platform governance. This article aims to reduce this uncertainty in two ways. First, it provides a theoretical framework on the benefits and risks of using agent-based modeling in platform governance, drawing on literature on platform risk assessment, the use of agent-based modeling in other fields, and anticipatory governance and evidence-based policymaking. Second, to validate this framework and clarify the conditions under which regulators can rely on evidence produced by agent-based models, the article reports the results of interviews with 18 regulators. The article finds that agent-based simulations have significant potential to complement other sources of evidence used in platform risk assessment. Their main added value is that they allow researchers and regulators to more easily, ethically, and independently explore the potential impacts of changes to platform design, and provide interactive and visual scenarios that concretize abstract platform dynamics.

title: “I even saw four deer grazing”: online-offline entanglements in the psilocybin pluriverse
authors: Michał Wanke, Paweł Matuszewski, Piotr Siuda
abstract: Purpose This paper aims to examine evolving practices and discourses surrounding psilocybin mushrooms on an online drug forum. Situating use within hybrid digital and ecological contexts, it traces shifts in user engagement before and after the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic to contextualize localized realities of the contemporary psychedelic renaissance. Design/methodology/approach Using an agential cut framework, the authors conducted a thematic analysis of approximately 3,000 qualitative posts from the largest Polish forum (mid-2000s–2023). The study tracks how forum users integrate digital tools (mapping, smartphone verification) with environmental field observations, mapping the online-offline entanglements and more-than-human relations of foraging. Findings Analysis reveals a post-2020 shift from immersive, metaphysical narratives toward predictive, scientifically informed and risk-managed practices. Building directly on critical drug scholarship regarding assemblage thinking and ontological multiplicity, the authors conceptualize the forum as an apparatus within a psychedelic pluriverse, where radically different versions of psilocybin simultaneously coexist, negotiate legitimacy and sustain themselves. Originality/value By integrating longitudinal forum analysis with a multispecies and pluriverse perspective, the study shows how digital platforms and embodied ecological practices jointly shape contemporary psychedelic practices, highlighting their relational, contingent and ethically informed character and demonstrating how shifts in discursive framings coincide with changing modes of ecological attention and risk reflexivity.

title: The role of altmetrics in citation emergence: a temporal approach
authors: José Luis Ortega, Evangelina Becerra Rodero, Sergio Castro Cortacero
abstract: This study aims to identify differences in the temporal distribution of altmetrics, detect associations between them, and determine which metrics have the greatest capacity to influence citation impact. Mentions and their appearance dates for 7,963 research publications were extracted from Altmetric.com. Several data mining techniques (transition matrices and association rules) and survival analysis methods (the Kaplan-Meier estimator, Cox proportional hazards models, and Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) models) were used to find longitudinal relationships between altmetrics and bibliographic citations. The results showed that there are two groups of metrics according to their temporal distribution. Attention metrics (social networks and digital media) are those that occur almost immediately after publication and have a short lifespan, while publication metrics, which come from edited publications (citations, Wikipedia, policy documents, patents), appear later and accumulate gradually over time. Social networks and digital media have greater interactions among themselves than publication metrics. Regarding the role of altmetrics in preceding citation impact, social networks have the ability to trigger the appearance of citations and shorten the time it takes for them to occur. Specifically, Research Highlights and Clinical Guidelines, two metrics associated with qualitative assessments, are those that most strongly influence the appearance of citations.

title: Consent to sharing shopping data for health research: a mixed-methods study
authors: Romana Burgess, Poppy Taylor, Kate Shiells, Mark Mumme, Andy Boyd, Anya Skatova
abstract: Introduction: Shopping data offer detailed, objective records of diet and lifestyle habits, providing a valuable but under-used resource for health research. While willingness to share personal data has been widely studied, no previous research has directly compared stated willingness with subsequent actual data sharing within the same cohort. Understanding whether willingness predicts actual consent, and how this varies by sociodemographic characteristics, motivations, and barriers, is essential to improve recruitment strategies and the representativeness of collected data. Methods: We employed a mixed-methods approach using the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). Quantitative analyses combined sociodemographic variables with responses from a 2018 survey assessing willingness to consent to shopping data linkage and outcomes from a 2023 loyalty card consent campaign. These were complemented by semi-structured interviews in 2019 exploring attitudes towards sharing shopping data. Results: In 2018, 60.7% of participants indicated willingness to share shopping data (n = 2142, 9230 invited). In 2023, actual consent—requiring provision of loyalty card details for five UK retailers (Sainsbury’s, Tesco, Boots, Morrisons, and Coop)—was obtained from 511 of 6170 invited participants. Demographic characteristics were broadly comparable across samples. Stated willingness was associated with higher odds of actual consent (adjusted OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.16–1.90), indicating that willingness is a meaningful predictor of subsequent data sharing. Sociodemographic characteristics and motivations (e.g., societal benefit, scientific interest) showed modest associations with both willingness and consent and moderated the willingness–consent relationship. Interviews reinforced the importance of trust in ALSPAC and clear communication about data use, while highlighting privacy concerns as minor barriers. Conclusion: This study provides the first within-cohort evidence that willingness to share shopping data predicts subsequent data sharing, albeit imperfectly. Willingness surveys can therefore inform recruitment planning, but additional strategies are required to improve realised consent rates. Addressing practical barriers, clarifying data use, and highlighting societal and scientific benefits may help narrow this gap; Targeted engagement, including through informative materials and outreach events, may help to reach groups less likely to consent. These insights can inform data collection in longitudinal cohorts and wider population samples.

title: Data Note: Linking Shopping Transaction Data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children
authors: Romana Burgess, Mark Mumme, Andy Boyd, Anya Skatova
abstract: Introduction: This data note describes the collection and linkage of participant’s Sainsburys shopping data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). Methods: Data were collected via informed participant consent and Subject Access Requests under UK GDPR, securely transferred and integrated into the ALSPAC database using unique study identifiers. Participant consultation and a technical pilot ensured transparent, acceptable, and secure linkage. Results: The dataset contains anonymised transactions for 244 participants, covering 658,375 item purchases between July 2014 and February 2024. Transactions include an item description, price, quantity, time, date, store location, and other variables. Items fall into 82 categories (e.g., produce) and 1002 subcategories (e.g., bananas). In terms of representativeness, the cohort is skewed towards females, consists of participants of similar age, and is predominantly made up of individuals with higher educational attainment and of White ethnicity. Conclusion: The linkage of shopping records complements ALSPACs extensive existing dataset, which comprises over 30 years of sociodemographic and health-related data.
